thread.title: What natural wine did you open today?

posts.name: Luca Giupponi
posts.raw:
Inspired by the great thread started by @Robert_Dentice and @Jayson_Cohen’s idea, I figured I would start this thread. I am by no means a natural wine expert but I’ve really enjoyed a lot of those I’ve tried. Please let’s not devolve into a thread trying to define natural wine! I’m hoping this thread will help forumites find new bottles to try. I've been on a huge German wine kick lately and I've been able to find a lot of great recommendations on this forum, for which I am grateful. Just tonight I got a chance to open this beautiful bottle from Kissinger:
  • 2022 Moritz Kissinger Weissburgunder - Germany, Rheinhessen (1/3/2025)
    Kissinger is of my favorite latest discoveries. This wine is a clear step up from the entry level “Null Ohm”, with noticeable vibrancy and concentration. The natty edge is intriguing, refreshing, energizing. Highly recommended.
![IMG_7851 Large|375x500](upload://lvm7WdPHnBvvLK21ARPJIICpuEu.jpeg)

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.raw:
Fantastic idea and what a perfect wine to start with!   Thank you.   The German wine scene is really evolving and growing in a good way.  I tasted with two fantastic growers in the Mosel last week that are in the natural camp - Thorsten Melsheimer and a new project Grandboise Weine.

I highly recommend trying all of the Melsheimer wines including the no SO2 wines.  

https://www.instagram.com/weingut_melsheimer/

And these are not yet imported into the U.S. but are available in Europe. I don't love Pet Nat but I did really like these. 

https://www.instagram.com/grandboisweine/

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.raw: Melsheimer has been biodynamic since 2013 and organic since 1995.  He has two lines one 0-0 and one with very low SO2.  He adds nothing in the winery and lets the wine ferment slowly.  The vineyard work is as labor intensive as any winery in the world due to the steepness.

posts.name: Michael Blasco
posts.raw:
![capture2|375x499](upload://AfYmjRvLlo6luMLh6PAsnEYtcem.jpeg)

Seems to be a hard question to define natural wine in 2025, but I hope this qualifies!

**2022 Chateau Cambon Brouilly**

Biodynamic, unsulfured, unfiltered. Wine is a joint project of Lapierre (whom I love) and Joseph Chamonard (whom I have never tried). This is classic Brouilly, classic bistro wine. It explodes with fresh crunchy red fruit (ripe strawberry dribbling down the chin) -- these flavors are why I love carbonic maceration. Palate is balanced acidity and silk tannins with round mouthfeel. It is not complex but it is wonderful in its simplicity with an intoxicating nose.

This wine is both natural (fresh, honest) but also beautifully refined (balanced, silky), without any weirdness.

posts.name: Edward Thompson
posts.raw:
HA! I actually just drafted up the start of a similar thread and came in to post only to find myself beat to it. Thank you, @Luca_Giupponi!

It's a huge category that is tricky to define. Sure,b iodynamic vineyard practices, natural yeast fermentation, a highly skeptical approach to sulphur and filtering are probably the basics, but that encompasses several wines not typically thought of as natural. *I guess Justice Stewart’s standard of “I know it when I see [drink] it” applies.*

While it certainly feels at times like there is forum consensus that natural wines are unserious, broadly flawed, and generally worth of derision rather than serious study, I also know that there are multitudes of delicious, interesting well-crafted examples that deserve our collective attention. The “natural” category being one of, if not the, fastest growing segment of wine and the mainstay of most wine lists at trendy new restaurants, it’s a not only a category that draws in many younger would be wine-conissuers, but is something even seasoned veterans should seek to understand more. Hopefully we can foster a balanced discussion focused on the epicurean value of these wines. Just as annoying as having some hipster try and gaslight you into thinking mouse is okay or desirable is having a whole world of juice dismissed out of hand. So hopefully discourse can not only highlights the diversity of the genre and broadens the horizons of all berserkers, but also acknowledges the potential inconsistencies and watch-outs that can happen as well. 

Should be a fun one!

Should be a fun one!

posts.name: Edward Thompson
posts.raw:
Here's what I've got tonight to go with some homemade Khao Soi.
![IMG_2388|396x500](upload://gEuwsHoE3N0HJxbcrfYwWoqL2JQ.jpeg)
An Alsatian blend from Christian Binner of mostly riesling & pinot gris seasoned with some gerwurztraminer, muscat, and auxerrois. Fermentation occurs in foudre. Labeled as white, but I'd say it straddles the line between white and orange personally. Decent amount of sediment, but not crazy. I stood it up for a few days before drinking, as is my want, and it poured clear. Its got a thick, oily feel but ample acidity keeps it zippy. Lots of tropical fruit on the nose but more apricot on the palate. Decent amount of floral spice as well. A hint of petrol and salinity as well. Great fun and something that easily holds its own in the company of pungent spicy food. Enjoyable to drink on it's own too. At $24, massive value here.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.raw:
Ok have I got a special wine and new discovery to kick off my first tasting note in this thread!  

This intersects on three themes that I have been talking about lately: 

**1.) New Producers in Burgundy.**  I am really excited about all of the new producers in Burgundy. Most have worked at top domains, many are new to Burgundy, many have at least one if not two feet in the natural camp and are typically making wines from the less heralded areas of burgundy. 

**2.) Simple Wines**.  I am enjoying the simple wines and new discoveries so much more these days.  

**3.) Natural Wines.**  I am guessing most would not think this was a "natural" wine if they did not know.  Once again I sat in a packed restaurant in NYC and looked around at a very stylish young crowd where every single table had wine on it, even in dry January.  

On to the wine:


**2022 Raphaelle Guyot - Irancy Le Baiser**

Let me tell you how great this wine is, my wife and I frequently order two bottles or when we run into friends or make friends order more than two but we never, never order two of the same bottle. This wine was so extraordinary we ordered two of the same wine!  We did share half of the second bottle with our neighbors : ) 

This wine was absolutely stunning. Translucent red. Beautiful aromatics.  The clarity and fidelity of the bright, pure, seductive Pinot flavors was just awe inspiring.  Renee and I just kept looking at each other and saying is this real!  Renee said it has to be a fruit night. We looked it up and it was flower night.  The wine is cerebral, emotional and simply life-affirming on every level.  There was not a hint of wood or manipulation it was just true to itself! This is everything I want in wine these days!  The second bottle did not quite have the fireworks of the first, was it us or the bottle?  I did buy 6 bottles this morning before posting this note :joy: 

Side note: I learned about Raphaëlle Guyot in Austin Texas.  I was there on a business trip and went into Richard Hargreaves (formerly of Majordomo) restaurant Underdog and was greeted by none other than MS Michael Engelmann and former Wine Director of The Modern, I last saw him working in Paris so was shocked to see him in Austin, I asked for a recommendation and this is the producer he recommended!  There is a big natural wine scene in Austin. 

Some background 

https://newcomerwines.com/collections/raphaelle-guyot?srsltid=AfmBOoo3ykLNwTP8Mr7LS05_dsEOvNFktmyI_foig0XWlE2ikdMGh52_

https://chambersstwines.com/blogs/articles/what-yonne-star-rises-raphaelle-guyots-stirring-burgundies

https://www.beckywasserman.com/domaines/raphaelle-guyot/

https://www.mfwwineco.com/raphaelle-guyot

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.raw:
![1000006123|375x500](upload://j434No8t0XMxmNV09UgygZTG3rg.jpeg)

Domaine Giachino took over this legendary Domaine from their friend Grissard in 2015. So this is only the second vintage by their hand. Besides this red, based on Mondeuse, they also make an interesting white Altesse under the label.

It has been a few years since I had the 2016. Really liked it at release.

It starts out well. Highly aromatic and high pitched. It has some very Beaujolais like sweetish carbonic fruit mixed with some darker Syrah like fruit tones. It also shows all kinds of spices, including some pepper, which instantly takes my mind to the more natural camp in the Northern Rhone. With air a bit of something vegetable/green shows up. A lot of stuff is going on, but it works. It is all wrapped in a light body with very little structure. High acidity and 12% abv.

I am really enjoying my first glass while cooking.

Then I get back to it, maybe 45 minutes after I openend it, and it has lost all its intensity. Same notes but dialed down to almost nothing.
Thought it might be closing down, but I wouldn't really expect that from a wine like this.
Anyways, as it is now very dull I decant it and follow it for some hours. It never returns and I give up.

I prefered it in its early youth. I have one bottle left. Will just keep it for 5-10 years to see what happens out of pure curiosity.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.raw:
**2022 Domaine Glinavos "Paleokerisio" Semi-Sparkling Orange** 

This is a fun one that I purchased on a whim.  $15 for a 500ml.  An orange sparkling wine made from an indigenous grape from Greece called Debina.   It was really complex and fascinating.  I love wines like this when you have a tough food pairing as I could see it going with a number of things. I also think it would work will with something like blue cheese or stinky cheeses that are impossible to pair.  It is has lot of skin ferment / orange wine spicy type flavors, plus a hint of sweetness and then it is sparkling!  Free Jazz wine!   I really enjoyed it and will buy it again if I am pairing wines for a dinner party. 

https://www.brooklynwineexchange.com/shop/product/domaine-glinavos-paleokerisio-semi-sparkling-orange-2018-500ml-1

Also made me think of my favorite Beck song!

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OuvR9d73OlI&t=9s

P-R was fairly rare at one point and now it seems they’re pretty ubiquitous. Their distribution network has changed and now they are on almost every wine list. 

They seem to be vehemently against seeing their wine at retail, but they have a lot of wine to sell, their Gevrey and Nuits plots are fairly large in size. Not sure how this is going work for them, but they have deep pockets so it might not matter.

The late release 09’s from a couple of years ago have been consistently very good. Likewise a recent 19 Suchots was top. Then an 18 Clos Goillotte was a mouse trap after thirty minutes.

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.raw: I worked for a company that imported it around 2005. Stunning wine. I introduced the owner of our company to Valette. We had Valentini and he walked out of the meeting with Valette saying Noly was a better wine than Valentini Treb. At that time, and I believe today, Valentini was more expensive but we had a hell of a time selling Noly to anyone. Most people recognized it was a special wine but I think people were afraid to pay up like that for Macon. Also, especially in those relatively early days of 'natural' a wine like that wasn't really easily received by hardcore Burgundy purists.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.raw:
I am going to cheat a little as I actually drank this wine a month ago.

But I love this domaines white wines and I think they are a very good introduction to Jura. Squeaky clean and is available and has not turned into super expensive unicorns. They can be found for less than 35€ in Europe and around 46$ in the US it seems (according to WS). Both **En Quatre Vis** cuvées are also really good (Chardonnay or Savagnin). They benefit a lot from 2-3 years cellaring, but can be enjoyed young. These are not wines raised in an oxidative manner.

So here is the note from my IG:

Marnes Blanches, Aux Bois, 2019. 

Tremendous Savagnin! 

These wines evovle at a pretty high pace, so no need to wait 10+ years. 

This cuvée usually develops a very bready note, very reminiscent of Champagne. This was no different. My wife called it at a distance, proclaiming it smelled like Champagne. On top of that you get everything lemon. Lemon curd and freshly pressed lemon juice. Sea salt and a mild salty cheese note. Apples and slighty unmature fresh pinapple. A little butter. Almonds. A mild hint of fresh fennel. High intensity. I love it! 

![1000005770|400x500](upload://9C7cU7KYfjhnr0NPDE3cvOcf7qE.webp)

Les Justices / Marie-Kurdziel Palsambleu 2021 
pnp, wrote my note about 30 mins after opening 
Dark fruit, plum, cassis, rich almost mushroomy earth, some minerals, and a hint of greenness on the finish. More full bodied/richer than expecting. Seems to be more brooding, but has the acid of the vintage. With air and warming up, seemed to lighten up the fruit profile a bit into cherry/redder fruit. Going to follow this over a few days, but my only bottle.

looked up the tech because I was curious, no fining/filtering/so2, from the Les Poyeux vineyard in sammur champigny. I don't think I'll be trying the Clos Rougeard version anytime soon so not sure how to compare (this was $40? compared to the CR $250 for current vintage), but fun to try something from a famous lieu dit.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
On the lovely geeky French wineberserkers board somebody posed the same question. The same user noted that the typical oxidative style is 'restrained' with this change. Response #203

["Ce clos Reyssier est issu des millésimes 12, 13 et 14 (3 fûts de chaque millésime) avec un fût de 16 et un fût de 18. Philippe Valette m’a dit qu’il faisait ça depuis quelques temps déjà pour sa consommation personnelle et qu’il a décidé de passer le pas cette année. Ça permet de mélanger des millésimes très tendus comme 13 et 14 à des millésimes plus riches comme 16 et 18."](https://www.lapassionduvin.com/bourgognebeaujolais/2689-domaine-valette?start=180#1191592)

I'll be going to the area in April, so if anyone can help me get a visit I'll be happy to ask again :innocent:
posts.cooked:
On the lovely geeky French wineberserkers board somebody posed the same question. The same user noted that the typical oxidative style is ‘restrained’ with this change. Response #203

“Ce clos Reyssier est issu des millésimes 12, 13 et 14 (3 fûts de chaque millésime) avec un fût de 16 et un fût de 18. Philippe Valette m’a dit qu’il faisait ça depuis quelques temps déjà pour sa consommation personnelle et qu’il a décidé de passer le pas cette année. Ça permet de mélanger des millésimes très tendus comme 13 et 14 à des millésimes plus riches comme 16 et 18.”

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
Last night in Paris and first time trying Zeroine wines. I heard mixed reviews so far and wanted to try for myself. This one didn't disappoint. No crazy complexity, but a delicious, clean wine that tastes even better with food.
  • 2021 Zeroine Vin de France Chacha - France, Vin de France (1/5/2025)
    Started out quite subtle with a strong lemon character on the nose with minerals, lime, and bright yellow fruit developing with air. High acidity on the palate with more lemon notes, green apple, citrus, and minerals with a nice finish that has a lingering salinity. Very nice, clean wine with clear Jura character. It started out very tart and acidic, but clearly has the fruit and stuffing to age. This should be even better in 5 years. 92-93+ (92 points)
![IMG_0561|375x500](upload://ht4quf3K2GrkTHg22mryQp68WX6.jpeg)
posts.cooked:

posts.name: Greg Gardner
posts.raw:
Last night we had the 2023 Matassa Ollla rouge.  11%, always a little spritzy on opening.  Gave it a good shake and that aspect goes away.  Unfortunately this bottle was a little bretty- not horrible but definitely distracting.  I'm not sure I'm a buyer on these going forward.  Especially since I find the Duchene 'La Luna' to be consistently clean and offering a gorgeously pure expression of old vine Grenache.  And the price point is about the same.  

A few nights ago we had the Le Puy “Marie-Cécile”, Vin de France Blanc.  This is 100% Semillon Bdx blanc and is just so damn good.  Really shows that 'zero zero' wines can be absolutely clean.  I wish someone other than Rosenthal brought these into the US, as they would probably be 15-20% less expensive.

posts.name: Edward Thompson
posts.raw:
![IMG_2496|439x499](upload://piXOTUi7xb1ZlstayP3LZSYa97u.jpeg)
I'm not sure it gets much more "natural" than this - a cloudy orange wine made of both Pinot Noir and Gerwurtztraminer courtesy of a young maker in Alsace, Catherine Riss. It's even got a funky label and a sassy name. I've had her wines before but never seen this one, or any other conferment from these grapes, so gave it a shot!

**Catherine Riss, "Pied de Nez," Alsace, FR - pinot noir & gerwurtraminer (2023)**
I was genuinely expecting a mousetrap or VA bomb when I ordered it, but was pleasantly surprised to find a quite clean and cohesive, if exuberant, wine instead. This one had all the fruits. Litterally all of them - mangoes, cherries, cider apples, pineapples - the whole lot. If you go looking for more, expect to find plenty of citrus peel, some oyster shell like salinity, and some green herbs. Maybe a hint of residual sugar, but generally quite bright and dry. Slightly viscous body but it didn't linger overlong in the mouth by any means.
I say this in the most endearing possible manner, but this is profoundly unserious, delicious, gulpable, food-friendly glou glou.  Superb fun!
posts.cooked:

posts.name: Maciej Kocan
posts.raw:
I don’t think I had the Cocus, only L’esprit Libre ‘22. 
From memory, L’L rather minimalistic, lithe, more focused, built around a mineral core. Raised in steel? LNM richer, yeasty, possibly some oak was involved? Two fine interpretations of Chenin.

I also had the Amor Fati, 100% Grolleau. That’s it, there is nothing left. If I get a chance I will buy again, despite rather steep pricing, but they are becoming quite scarce, usually bundled with less in demand names.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
We opened some natural wine icons over this past weekend, celebrating my birthday :partying_face: ![IMG_0602|375x500, 100%](upload://kOaqsSDmVyfnYSeovAFUBsIFilg.jpeg)
  • 2015 Domaine des Miroirs (Kenjiro Kagami) Côtes du Jura Berceau - France, Jura, Côtes du Jura (1/18/2025)
    My first experience with Miroirs. The nose was great and quite intense with lots going on. Notes included lemon, peach, riper fruit, maybe a hint of pineapples, and minerals. High acidity on the palate with lots of fruit flavors, stone fruit, lemon, orange, more minerals, dry extract and a hint of reduction that blew off with air. Long finish. Excellent wine that is deep and complex, but perhaps not the prettiest example of Jura Chardonnay, as it came off as surprisingly muscular. I wouldn't say this wasn't a great experience, but expectations may have been unreasonably high. (95 points)
![IMG_0607|375x500](upload://wqpOmFotuRHTEu8wgLhYZ7OWjf3.jpeg)
  • 2005 Jean-Marc Brignot Trésor d'Aiglepierre - France, Vin de France (1/18/2025)
    Super complex nose with lots of citrus, oxidation, almonds, noticeable VA, fragrant flowers, and minerals. Acidity is quite high on the palate with citrus, lemon, crushed rocks, and more nutty notes on the palate. Long finish. Gorgeous wine that's super unique and complex. A joy to sip (slowly and deliberately). (96 points)
  • 2022 Domaine Takahiko Pinot Noir Nana-Tsu-Mori - Japan, Hokkaido (1/18/2025)
    Interesting nose with quite a bit of volatile notes, red cherries, and flowers. Acidity is quite high and the palate feels very tart and maybe a bit underripe with notes of red cherries, pomegranate, orange, and a hint of forest notes. Good finish. A nice wine, but clearly behind the other vintages I had (21 and 19). It's enjoyable but based on this bottle, I wouldn't chase it. It's also interesting that I get reminded of different regions every time I drink Takahiko: the 19 tasted like Jura, the 21 like Burgundy, and the 22 more like Auvergne... (92 points)

Here I am to lowering the average :face_in_clouds:

![IMG_8139|375x500](upload://ksYygEZFsnMtmP55INv7lvqNeqO.jpeg)


**Melsheimer - Molun 1143**

I saw @Robert_Dentice posting about it, besides a few shops I know in Europe carry the wines. Decided to give it a shot. 

Fresh and bright Riesling with good depth and balance.

Deep and herby nose with petrol and pears, and the pears follow in the first sip as a dominant note with a backdrop of white ripe fruits. While it is very dry, there's absolutely some sugar left but it's very well balanced with the acidity, which, to my taste, could be a little stronger. It's a very nice and well-composed Riesling that I'd be happy to pick up again.

posts.raw:
To be honest, there were so many wines tasted at one go that it's impossible to remember any single wines without my notes (which are not at hand now). There wasn't any consistent theme which were good or which were not; some whites were better than others, the same with reds. 

Although the sparkling wines were pretty meh. Those I'd skip without a second thought.

posts.raw:
I opened a 2021 Christian Ducroux Exspectatia on Monday to have over a few nights. On day one it showed the lovely crunchy and tart fruit I enjoy from Ducroux, with a slight effervescence on the palate. Sadly, it became increasingly mousy and by last night (day 3), undrinkable. I've had the same problem with 2018 Herve Souhaut La Souteronne, though subsequent vintages have been better. This bums me out as I love Ducroux's wines.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
![IMG_0649|375x500](upload://fq3yYE4ufvDPWWh3B0ltZNM7aM3.jpeg) Two hyped up natural rose wines. The Glandien was actually better than I expected, but the Horees was a bit of a letdown...
  • 2023 Maison Glandien l'Ouverture Rosé - France, Vin de France (1/30/2025)
    Nice nose with some red berries, grapefruit, minerals, and some floral notes. Good acidity with similar flavor profile. Medium finish. This is a nice rose, well rounded and balanced but not overly complex or deep. (90 points)
  • 2023 Les Horées Bourgogne Bonheur Rosé - France, Burgundy, Bourgogne (1/30/2025)
    The nose felt quite ripe with lots of strawberry, cherry, and slightly jammy notes. The wine is a bit leaner and tarter on the palate with flavors of orange, citrus, zest, and more strawberries and cherries. Medium finish. I wasn't very impressed by this, but it grew on me on day 2. Still, was expecting more... (89 points)

posts.name: Glenn Bienstock
posts.raw:
I see notes on mostly European wines. Here is one from California.

2022 Conduit Syrah, Gold Dust Vineyard, Fair Play, El Dorado County. Unfined and unfiltered, low sulfur addition. 22 year old vines. Planted on decomposed granite, this wine is very minerally and composed on a medium bodied frame. Flavors of blackberry and some bacon fat with a hint of raspberry. Excellent lip smacking texture with 13% ABV and a long cascading finish. No funkiness and evolving over several hours. A wine that intrigues changes in the glass, stimulates the tastebuds and senses and inspires curiosity, it is a very nice match with Merguez lamb sausage on the Weber.

My only criticism is a shy nose that hopefully should fill out with some bottle age.

https://www.conduitwine.com/store/p/estatesyrah

![1000004719|225x500](upload://e8AHGFJuN2ntVPMb687WZeWouxl.jpeg)

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.raw:
Haroon Rahimi Architecte (no vintage was listed)


I mentioned this wine in the Food and Wine section.  Mika friend and owner of Les Enfants du Marche sent word ahead that I needed to try it.  Of course without hesitation I said yes!  All I knew was that it was Pinot from Alsace!  It was a stunning soulful wine that grabbed my attention.  It had a shocking and lovely hit of Rose with the silky texture of a high end Pinot but with a lively natural wine energy.  And it was clean, very clean!   But the hint of Rose was what took it from a very good wine to a great wine!    

I discovered after the fact it is co-fermented with Rose pedals! F/#$ I have always poo pooed co-ferments, well I guess the joke is on me. 

If anyone knows where to get these wines I am all ears. It reminds me of my first Xavier Caillard wine! 

https://www.wine-searcher.com/m/2024/11/a-winemakers-journey-from-afghanistan-to-alsace

https://www.raisin.digital/en/artisans/natural-winemakers/haroon-rahimi-harjane-27091/wines/whites/

And it turns out the wine is made in miniscule quantities by an Afghan war refugee who is living in Alsace.   WOW!

posts.name: Edward Thompson
posts.raw:
![IMG_2640|375x500](upload://fGne1zSAF6ucD0NRFSV060Sjne7.jpeg)
Absolutely superb wine. Perhaps the best I’ve had in 2025 so far. Pulled the cork was instantly greeted with intense florals and ripe fruit - jasmine, magnolia, ylang ylang, peach, nectarine, and mango. Similar flavors on the palette with the addition of some herbs - sorrel, tarragon, and thyme - and lots of flinty minerality. Luxurious mouth filling texture matched by wonderfully potent acidity. Other than a hint of fizz that quickly calmed down, an extremely clean wine. Hauntingly delicious. I will be going out of my way to find more of this.

Today it’s all Greek to me.
![IMG_0256|375x500](upload://cqPuXrvJI6aE4qEx72uZ0cmX7ub.jpeg)

Another Greek Rose, from an unheard of variety. No intervention, cloudy. More strawberry juice, rose petals, watermelon,  herbs. Good acidity, juicy, medium body. Not unlike the recent (possibly flawed?) Sylvain Pataille. Between solid and good.

![IMG_0257|375x500](upload://88LVuVfztqhYoj36Pw5fhBaJF2C.jpeg)

100% Assyrtiko now. All kinds of citrus fruit, bananas(?), hazelnuts, herbal honey, oak. Dense and viscous. Little in common with Santorini, more like Northern Rhone Whites, but with more nerve. A bit stolid yet solid (sorry).

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_8272|375x500](upload://iIU5XdKYaKsWPr0zi5yQ8TA9qXJ.jpeg)

**Domaine Labet Fleur de Savagnin 2018**
This deeply particular smell that Savagnin has… After 12 hours the wine is open for business and has manifested itself with that strong sesame, apple, and acacia smell. The first sip is a battle between malo, oxidation, and acidity. Pears are the main dominant fruit here. The heat of the vintage is shining through. Amazing. I have one bottle left and will leave it for 8 years for the heck of it. 

**Domaine Rietsch - Quand le Chat n'est Pas la 2020**
Roses, a bit of VA, and a creamy note. Strawberry, and a good dose of tannins and acidity. Love these Pinot Gris macerations. Is it red or orange? I don't care. Fantastic value for the money at 20€. 

I mean the Labet even more so for the 23€ I paid, but those days are long gone now…

posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.raw:
![image|499x499](upload://naS6CJlrLZTMAJqHZkkbGRqbKFT.jpeg)
  • 2018 COS Vittoria Delle Fontane - Italy, Sicily, Vittoria DOC (3.2.2025)
    Not a shy nose here with tons of herbs and bouillon swarming from the glass. Notably spicy too with a smoky and earthy feel and some VA, naturally. One thing that doesn’t jump out though is fruit, at least not the ripe kind. On the palate it has gained some weight over a couple of hours, having been on the light side early on. Sports a low 12.5% ABV which is good for one’s sleep quality but I wonder if just a pinch more of ripeness would be beneficial here. Still, having been breathing some this has developed a good deal of textural richness and weight and is plenty appealing in that sense. A different vintage might produce a more intriguing fruit expression, which would elevate this to a level I have come to expect from COS’s reds. Even then, this is a perfectly decent Monday-night tipple.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
Received a "Not Drinking Poison" newsletter today, with a link to a more recent but very short interview with Legeron of RAW fair. I think it's very good to see the discussion inside this branch of the industry, but I wonder in how many cases it's about the efforts of these skilled 0/0 winemakers, and not just the economic and social changes discussed in many other threads here on the board. 

https://swurlmedia.co/interviews/raw-wines-isabelle-legeron-on-the-evolution-of-the-wine-fair

Ayscough continues;

> “ I think we really need to focus more heavily on delivering wines that are drinkable, well crafted, and balanced. In the world of natural wine, it should no longer be acceptable to pour wines that are mousy, unbalanced, or fragile, and expect that the market is going to buy them,” says Legeron. “I think we’re seeing this in all markets: people are leaning away from some of the crazier bottles and returning to more controlled styles.”
> 
> A journalist might well have picked at that word, *control* . How, exactly, do you control a wine? And what aspects of a wine’s profile might make a consumer applaud a wine’s *controlled* quality?
> 
> Perhaps more pertinently, given widespread public ignorance of viticulture and the (complex but still fairly straightforward) processes of natural winemaking, why is it that wine must be *controlled* to conform to the market’s demands, and not vice versa?
> 
> For a renowned natural wine writer and events organizer to insist that natural wine conform to market demands is to highlight the limitations, if not the utter superfluity, of her own public platform. If many excellent masterful natural vignerons are today having difficulty selling their excellent masterful natural wines, it is rather too easy to blame unnamed but implicitly lazy or careless natural winemakers releasing bad natural wines. I would instead point to the public figures exacerbating public wine miseducation by insisting that truly natural wines should taste just like filtered and sulfited “natural” wines.
posts.cooked:
Received a “Not Drinking Poison” newsletter today, with a link to a more recent but very short interview with Legeron of RAW fair. I think it’s very good to see the discussion inside this branch of the industry, but I wonder in how many cases it’s about the efforts of these skilled 0/0 winemakers, and not just the economic and social changes discussed in many other threads here on the board.

SWURL

RAW WINE’s Isabelle Legeron On The Evolution Of The Wine Fair

The highly credentialed Master of Wine is all about transparency.

Ayscough continues;

“ I think we really need to focus more heavily on delivering wines that are drinkable, well crafted, and balanced. In the world of natural wine, it should no longer be acceptable to pour wines that are mousy, unbalanced, or fragile, and expect that the market is going to buy them,” says Legeron. “I think we’re seeing this in all markets: people are leaning away from some of the crazier bottles and returning to more controlled styles.”

A journalist might well have picked at that word, control . How, exactly, do you control a wine? And what aspects of a wine’s profile might make a consumer applaud a wine’s controlled quality?

Perhaps more pertinently, given widespread public ignorance of viticulture and the (complex but still fairly straightforward) processes of natural winemaking, why is it that wine must be controlled to conform to the market’s demands, and not vice versa?

For a renowned natural wine writer and events organizer to insist that natural wine conform to market demands is to highlight the limitations, if not the utter superfluity, of her own public platform. If many excellent masterful natural vignerons are today having difficulty selling their excellent masterful natural wines, it is rather too easy to blame unnamed but implicitly lazy or careless natural winemakers releasing bad natural wines. I would instead point to the public figures exacerbating public wine miseducation by insisting that truly natural wines should taste just like filtered and sulfited “natural” wines.

Had a really outstanding wine last night that sadly was only made for a short time.  The **2018 Closerie du Chene from Famille Grebaut.**  The backstory is that the vineyard was purchased by Bertrand Grebaut, Chef of Septime, parents upon their retirement. Shortly after they purchased Christian died.  His Mother, Catherine, carried on converting the vineyard to organic and made wine for a few years.  It was super fresh, vivid and uplifting while still maintaining Bordeaux character.  I am searching for more this morning.  Bridges is pouring it by the glass. Fascinating wine. 

https://www.selectionmassale.com/closerie-du-chene.html

posts.name: Mariano Chiaramonte
posts.raw:
I'm going to pretend that I did not read the "today" portion of the topic :see_no_evil:

![2E4ED7D2-4233-460E-87DE-09E4C3FA3BB0|500x500](upload://7n2bwXZGyTxHvwu5LHngibAudJf.jpeg)

**Filed recordings Freddo**.
I don't remember the vintage, but I think it doesn't matter, this "chillable" red wine is great as an aperitive, that's about it.

**2020 Nicolò Grippaldi Sicilia Le Domeniche di Teo**
76% Nerello Mascalese, 24% Nero d'Avola. As far as I know this is the only producer in the Enna (not to be confused with Etna) region of Sicily. Really interesting wine, it has that vibrant red fruit form NM with a darker touch of NdA and a touch of herbal tea that reminds me a little bit of COS. The wine is clean with no distinguishable natty character. This is a producer that I'll follow up closely.

**2017 Domaine Les Cocottes Bourgogne Hautes-Côtes de Beaune Les Equeillons**
Quick background story about this wine: In 2020, a distributor friend of mine incorporate this wine into his portfolio after tasting it at the winery in Burgundy. He was shocked when the wine arrived in Miami because it was nothing close to the one he tasted across the pond, it was funky, a bit disjointed and imposible to sell to his customers (mostly posh restaurants), so he offers it to a group of friends at $17 per bottle. I bought 3 cases.
It turns out that during a 2-month period that wine stabilized and went back to the original form of a clean and vibrant burg with no natty character whatsoever, and during this 5-year period since we bought it, has been evolving in the right direction and it's drinking like a good village lever burg. Sometimes you win one :grinning:

**2018 Harrington Eros III Cep Sumu Kaw Vineyard**
While still enjoyable, it feels like it's starting to decline, and drinks like a glou-glou natural wine with little typicity.

**2021 Idlewild Barbera Lost Hills Ranch**
The best domestic Barbera that I've tried so far and better that many Italian counterparts. Not only offers that vibrant purple fruit with piercing acidity so typical of the variety, but also a tannic structure that is difficult to find in Barbera.

**2021 Marcel Lapierre Morgon (S)**
Still in its infancy, but you can get a preview of the 2021 vintage greatness. This should evolve into something special.

  • 2022 Vin Noé Saint-Aubin 1er Cru La Combe Shadows - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Saint-Aubin 1er Cru (2/28/2025)
    Nice nose with a mix of minerals and fruit, apple notes, lemon, citrus, zest, and a hint of flowers. Great acidity on the palate with similar flavors, more lemon and bright yellow fruit, and slightly saline towards the finish. I quite enjoyed this, the flavor profile is bright and interesting and there is good precision. For the price, however, I would expect a bit more complexity and depth, but that may develop with time. 92+ (92 points)

posts.raw:
2010 Ganevat Vignes de Mon Pere. 

Only the second bottle I’ve drunk (with my wife over dinner) as opposed to a small tasting pour. This was more restrained and ‘normal’ than the 2008. Laser acidity, great mineral depth, very long, but elegant and fine. Very little oxidation and almost impossible to belive it was in barrel for 14 years. A treat.

Nothing like the JM Vincent. Not much complexity but great bright fruit and lively acidity. Easy drinking in a hazy unfiltered way.

![IMG_0850|375x500](upload://wTRGK9zkIaGOVx3b4T5qb71O97P.jpeg) We can't just let Russell drink and post about all the good stuff :rofl: I haven't been able to drink as much wine since we just had our first child, but some good friends came over last night and brought us food, so we opened this (plus 2002 Krug, which was also delicious).
  • 2021 Les Horées Bourgogne Le Vieux Sage - France, Burgundy, Bourgogne (3/8/2025)
    Nice nose with good amount of depth and notes of red fruit, distinct pine needles, red cherries, raspberries, and a hint of forest floor. Quite high acidity on the palate with tart cherry flavors and more red berries. Nice finish. This is a lovely wine that is drinking well although it's obviously still very primary. Despite how easy drinking and delicious it is right now, I felt this was actually quite serious and built to age for at least a decade or more. 93-94+ (94 points)

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
Julie Balagny was my first love (okay, maybe my second after Foillard) in Beaujolais. I had quite a few of her wines over the years and still have a decent amount in my cellar, but they've become increasingly hard to open. Today was a perfect opportunity and this didn't disappoint one bit. Emotional wine! ![IMG_0867|375x500](upload://o4ZRFhBgBRYXPFeQ9Dsf0Zc9Qdt.jpeg)
  • 2018 Julie Balagny Fleurie en Rémont - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Fleurie (3/11/2025)
    Lovely and fragrant nose that started out with a good dose of VA, but that dissipated with air in the glass. Notes of crunchy red berries, red cherries, strawberry, a bit of herbs and a small hint of cedar. High acidity with lots of tart fruit, some tertiary flavors of forest floor, and pomegranate on the palate. Nice finish that lingers for quite some time. The tart fruit and acidity hide the 14% abv really well, I would never have guessed it was that high. Overall, just a gorgeous natural wine. Nothing too serious, but by no means a simple wine. Definitely has that "vivant" quality to it. These are precious bottles now... (93 points)
posts.cooked:

Notes of crunchy red berries, red cherries, strawberry, a bit of herbs and a small hint of cedar. High acidity with lots of tart fruit, some tertiary flavors of forest floor, and pomegranate on the palate. Nice finish that lingers for quite some time. The tart fruit and acidity hide the 14% abv really well, I would never have guessed it was that high. Overall, just a gorgeous natural wine. Nothing too serious, but by no means a simple wine. Definitely has that “vivant” quality to it. These are precious bottles now… (93 points)

it’s exactly what makes Balagny’s wines so special. That tart fruit + acidity combo sounds super refreshing. Wish I had a bottle to pop open right now!

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
![IMG_0884|375x500](upload://qLPztXR0vUgGx8ZNbSDaub6ZVaJ.jpeg) Two lovely natural wines from last night. Ganevat showed very well. The Cavarodes was also very good, but perhaps not quite as complex as some of the older reds I had from the domaine.
  • 2012 Jean-François Ganevat Grusse en Billat - France, Jura, Côtes du Jura (3/13/2025)
    Nice nose that picked up a LOT of weight with air. Notes of stone fruit, lemon, minerals, hay, herbs, and a hint of smoke. Nice acidity with lemon and lime notes, lots of minerals, salinity, and tart peach on the palate. Good finish. Lovely Chardonnay with good complexity. Compared to Ganevat’s higher end cuvées, this maybe misses a bit of complexity and length, but it’s so good on its own… (93 points)
  • 2015 Domaine des Cavarodes (Etienne Thiébaud) Arbois Trousseau de Messagelin - France, Jura, Arbois (3/13/2025)
    Nice nose with notes of cherries, forest floor, a bit of brett / barnyard, and raspberries. There’s a little effervescence on the palate that never dissipated, good acidity, tart red fruit, more cherries, and earthy flavors. Good finish. Very nice wine, perhaps a bit less nuanced than some of the older Cavarodes reds (especially the Poulsards) I’ve had, though. (91 points)

posts.name: Tanner Schmucker
posts.raw:
Thank you all for the suggestions! I’ll hold onto it for a few more years, then share it with people who I know will appreciate it.

When it comes to zero sulfur wines, I am curious how some producers can consistently produce high quality, relatively clean/not faulty wine whereas others are littered with faults. I know it’s a scale, and even some of the top zero-sulfur wines contain some VA and other resulting characteristics, but from what I’ve read the Domaine Ganevat wines are all zero sulfur while still performing at the top level (particularly the whites it seems). Another good example would be Lapierre Morgon (the zero sulfur one). 

That’s not to say they are always consistent, judging from Otto’s several Ganevat (and other Jura) tasting notes. However I wonder what the main variables are which make zero sulfur wines able to age gracefully for 5-10 years or more. Is it the high acidity of the wine? The age of the vines or quality of the grapes, leading to numerous other variables?  Cleanliness of the winery itself?

And would the wines be “improved” with a small dose of sulfur? Or would it take away something in the wine? 

Sorry, it’s something I’ve been thinking about lately while exploring wines with differing amounts of sulfur added. The consensus seems to be that using a tiny amount of sulfur to stabilize the wine, but not too much, is ideal. Luckily there are wines out there produced both ways, such as the aforementioned Lapierre Morgon (the “S” version having sulfur added if I remember correctly), so I suppose you can get a sense of what the difference might be. I haven’t tried a zero-sulfur and minimal-sulfur wine side by side before, which might be a good place to start.

Sorry for the tangent and for any of my information that might be incorrect. It’s something I’ve been thinking about lately, especially since I work with a lot of “natural wines” at work (ITB).

Amazing wines for lunch at one of our favorite locale restaurants. Also had a mind blowing bottle of 1988 Dom Perignon, but I guess that doesn't qualify for this thread ;) ![IMG_0893|375x500](upload://o9a81WnwVQI5QSQv6iBbVZAj6he.jpeg)
  • 2018 Domaine Bizot Marsannay Clos du Roy - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Marsannay (3/15/2025)
    Gorgeous wine on the nose with perfumed notes of darker fruit, cherries, spices, cloves, earth, and forest floor. Good acidity with a bit of tannic structure on the palate and flavors of strawberries, darker cherries, and more forest and underwood. There's a lot going on and the flavors are delineated and quite complex. Nice and lingering finish. Super delicious, serious wine that is drinking well but should ideally age for another 7-10+ years. I don't recall having anything remotely close in quality from Marsannay. 94++ (94 points)

posts.name: Edward Thompson
posts.raw:
![IMG_3004|375x500](upload://oa7nePE4BdMpjI9hv49EcNpsLxQ.jpeg)
It’s rare that I’m totally charmed by Riesling - I usually enjoy them more on an academic level than an emotional one if that makes any sense at all. This is different. Certainly the most “natural” Riesling I’ve had with a heavy dose of VA and a whiff of something that for lack of a better vocabulary I will have to just call funk. But it’s well integrated and energetic rather than detracting. Oodles citrus and pale stone fruits, bursting with bright acidity, and yet carrying a subtly yeasty character I don’t usually associate with Riesling. I’d have to look at the spec sheet, but I bet from the mouthfeel it’s carrying a faint hint of residual sugar too.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
[quote="T.Schmucker, post:330, topic:330008"]
Is it the high acidity of the wine? The age of the vines or quality of the grapes, leading to numerous other variables? Cleanliness of the winery itself?
[/quote]

All are important factors. When I read stories of how the chickens run freely from garden to chai at Metras I can't help but think they're important factors of this voluntary game of roulette I choose to indulge in. On the other hand, Cornelissen supposedly runs a very clean shop and has now switched over to a little sulphites added, but his wines can also be tainted.

Maybe it's worth doing a comparison yes. Buronfosse doesn't use sulphites at all as far as I'm aware, so maybe doing a comparison with Ganevat and Labet would be interesting as they all share the Varron field I believe. Labet adds a little sulphites from time to time. Ganevats' use seems to be hard to establish— although he states that the reds see absolutely no added sulphites, which to me just seem a bit crazy. Labet has started bottling red Pinots with these labels; 
"Vin sensible á l’air, à boire dès l’ouverture"
![image|375x500](upload://buDLot3AheuwLEdYpmkH7FxkQbY.jpeg)


In the Alsace I can also recommend trying Schuellers Eichberg GC or Meyers Muenchberg GC next to a conventional maker.

![IMG_0900|375x500](upload://vRyAGr5ydji0CqFXR6HInbYroSq.jpeg) A bit of a perplexing wine. Could smell this all day, though...
  • 2014 Gerard Schueller Pinot Noir LN012 - France, Alsace (3/16/2025)
    First experience with Schueller's Pinots. The nose is fantastic! Layers of fruit, flowers, a hint of baking spices, blood orange, pine needles, and tart berries. The palate, on the other hand, is predominantly tart and acidic. Once you get past that tartness, there are strawberries, pomegranate, tart cherries, and earthy notes. Good finish. I'm not really sure what to think about this. I absolutely loved the nose (and would probably rate it 94-95) but the palate is too much of a contrast and doesn't have the depth (probably a 88-89). (90 points)

posts.name: Edward Thompson
posts.raw:
![IMG_3021|375x500](upload://A6rXyPmX8fhlFv5tya2TcpljaoU.jpeg)
Pierre Mang, Les Voisins, 2022. Honestly this was a total dud for me. My wife refused to drink it, and I found it off putting enough to put a cork in it after a class and move onto something else. Wasn’t cork taint and didn’t behave like mouse (obvious retro nasally but not before drinking). Based on the smell and effect I’d say some foul variant of reduction, borderline butyric, sulfuric, and putrid meaty flavors with insane VA to top it off. Underneath that was some beautiful honeyed fruit and floral notes, but tough to look past the off putting notes. A little less offensive on day 2. Day 3 quite a lot less offensive but overtly vinegary. Not sure what fault I just walked into here and not sure if it’s just this wine or a bad bottle. But not eager to try again, especially at nearly $50…

    Impressive nose with lots of red and dark cherries, wild berries, herbal notes, and maybe a tiny hint of cola. Bright acidity and structural tannins frame the palate with more primary fruit character, strawberries, cherries, and a hint of earthiness. Long finish. This is a very young but potentially great wine. Still primary and fruit driven, but with good depth and complexity that’s starting to emerge. I would hold for 7+ years. 95+ (95 points)

I opened and decanted and then followed it for a few hours. Probably the right treatment because it got a bit more expressive with air, but the structure also came out more towards the end. It was enjoyable throughout.

I’ve generally had great experiences with Envinate, but the wines can have quite a bit of VA, so I tend to always decant them to help that blow off a bit… (this one didn’t have any excessive amounts, though)

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
Three really great natural wines from last night: ![IMG_1009|375x500](upload://rdRqbPJCZLHxWp9BFWAKHZn40oA.jpeg)
  • 2019 Jean-François Ganevat Côtes du Jura Vieilles Vignes Les Chalasses - France, Jura, Côtes du Jura (3/28/2025)
    Lovely although very youthful nose with a slight lactic note that dissipated quickly after opening. Followed by notes of lemon, citrus, a hint of smoke, and crushed rocks. High acidity on the palate with a distinct saline quality, lots of minerals, more lemon and citrus, and green apple flavors. Long finish. This is still a bit coiled up, but really good already. Should be outstanding in 5+ years. 95++ (95 points)
  • 2018 Renaud Bruyère & Adeline Houillon Arbois Pupillin Chardonnay Vieilles Vignes - France, Jura, Arbois Pupillin (3/28/2025)
    Beautiful nose with lots of fruit, apple, lemon, spices, and maybe a hint of stone fruit. Quite high acidity on the palate with flavors of lemon juice and zest, minerals, peach skin, and lovely precision. Long finish. This was excellent! Super complex and interesting with lots of depth and nice balance between acidity and citrus notes and riper and richer fruit. (96 points)
  • 2020 Non Progredi Est Regredi (Antonio Quari) Ladoix Les Briquottes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Ladoix (3/28/2025)
    Very fragrant nose with obvious stem influence, floral notes, red cherries, a hint of spices, and some wild strawberry that emerged with air. High acidity on the palate with tart fruit, raspberries, red cherries, and blood orange flavors. Nice finish. This is quite a light and very tart wine, but with good depth. I really enjoyed this and it's drinking beautifully right now. Would be interesting to see how this ages. 94+ (94 points)

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
![IMG_1018|375x500](upload://47FfIli0aJ5Q1f03LGMms7tv4Nh.jpeg) I haven't been the biggest fan of Bonnezeaux since they can be too ripe and have RS, but this was quite nice, although too young...
  • 2019 Château de Bonnezeaux La Montagne - France, Loire Valley, Vin de France (3/30/2025)
    The nose had a little bit of noticeable VA, but that blew off about an hour after opening. Notes of apples, minerals, peach, a hint of smoke, and lemon zest. Nice acidity on the palate with quite a bit of texture and structure, more apple flavors, stone fruit, pith, and minerals. Good finish. This is quite lovely and a rather big, serious wine. I feel like it's a bit coiled up at the moment and not showing everything. If there's a bit more complexity coming through with aging, this might turn out terrific. Hold for at least 5-7 years. 92++ (92 points)

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
Very lucky to taste some icons last night :star_struck: The whites were spectacular! ![IMG_1034|375x500](upload://vaQueX0yHtWzMdKXWQLv3i78VtH.jpeg) ![IMG_1036|375x500](upload://tGgkzi5C8H6MOj4jak8kJndY6Gq.jpeg)
  • 2014 Emmanuel Houillon (Maison Pierre Overnoy) Chardonnay Arbois Pupillin - France, Jura, Arbois Pupillin (4/1/2025)
    Gorgeous nose with lots of minerals, citrus, lime, and a hint of oxidation that actually became less pronounced with air. Laser sharp acidity on the palate with lemon, apple, minerals, and a hint of riper stone fruit notes. Long finish. Wow, what a wine! It started out great but kept improving and improving with time in the glass, gained intensity and power without ever losing its sharpness. Outstanding! (97 points)
  • 2015 I. et S. Bernaudeau Blanc Les Nourrissons - France, Vin de France (4/1/2025)
    This was even better than the last bottle! Beautiful nose with high precision, notes of lemon, citrus, and green apples. Lots of minerals on the palate with great acidity, linear mouthfeel and more citrus notes. Long finish. Amazing wine! This opened up really well over a few hours and showed more and more depth as time went on. (96 points)
  • 2009 Jean Foillard Morgon Cuvée 3.14 - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Morgon (4/1/2025)
    Great nose with quite a bit of oomph, lots of cherries, violets, other flowers, and forest notes. Nice acidity on the palate with good depth, darker cherries, strawberries, and underwood. Nice and lingering finish. Really lovely wine, but definitely on the bigger, darker, and richer side of Cuvée 3.14. Not quite as subtle and elegant as the 13 or 16 and for me personally a small step behind those vintages. Still has many years if not decades left, though... (94 points)

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
Had these two beautiful bottles over the weekend. If anyone thinks natural wines can't age, they're wrong ;) ![IMG_1074|375x500](upload://lq6cWuVllUlyMscFKsCvnzV2ub3.jpeg)
  • 2002 Emmanuel Houillon (Maison Pierre Overnoy) Chardonnay Arbois Pupillin - France, Jura, Arbois Pupillin (4/5/2025)
    This changed quite drastically with air. It started out nice, but not super clean, with a slightly musty and funky note on the palate. We decided to decant and after about an hour or two, this was spectacular! Very complex on the nose with lemon, dried flowers, apple, and a hint of nuts. Great acidity on the palate with layered notes of orange zest, a hint of dairy, green apple, and some almonds. Long finish. A really great, cerebral wine that demands your attention. (96 points)
  • 2003 Yvon Métras Fleurie L'Ultime - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Fleurie (4/5/2025)
    Gorgeous nose with lots of red fruit, cherries, earth, and a tiny hint of brett. Good acidity on the palate with a distinct wild strawberry note, sous bois, a bit of flowers, blood orange, and lots of red cherries. Long finish. This was excellent! Complex and deep with some tertiary notes showing its age, but so much lively fruit left, this can continue to age for decades. A testament to what Gamay can deliver. We hypothesized this tastes a bit like a blend between a higher end Burgundy and an Overnoy Poulsard. Wow! (96 points)

What bottle of wine did you open today? (Part 2) WINE TALK
[20250408_224656] Domaine Blanc Plume Rouge Barrique 2020 Second time I have this wine, second time I’m charmed. I’ll post on the nutty natty thread as well as this is biodynamic. Raphaël makes a rouge béton (concrete) and a rouge barrique (oak). Cabernet Franc, Carignan and Merlot. The nose has that slight green bell pepper CF note but with the Merlot berries underneath it. Very fond of the nose. The palate follows suit with plenty of red cherries and spices and all that savoury herbal/gua…

Very good.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_8627|375x500](upload://atnltLBOAUp6cu4iC5aAI5v3rVC.jpeg)

**Bruno Schueller Riesling Pfersigberg 'H' 1999**
Toffee and peach melba yoghurt with a slight hint of petrol. The acidity is very well integrated but at the lighter end of the spectrum because of the age. The fruit must have been very ripe at harvest because there’s a certain sweetness still there, despite it being dry. Lovely

**Flavien Nowack Les Bauchets '19**
Bruised red apple, cinnamon and ground coriander seed with a healthy dose of acidity, and a long finish. Fruits dominating are red apple, preserved lemon and a touch of cherry. Not the most complex but a very beautiful vinous champagne for the price. Everyone at the table loved it. Curious to see how it holds up tonight.

**Clos Roche Blanche La Closerie 1999**
Brett and a cool herb vibe with dill and mint. Dark red fruits with a touch of dark chocolate. It’s really amazing it’s so alive and kicking at this age. Strong tannins and long finish. Broad shouldered wine. Very very good.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_8679|375x500](upload://n7da0dXIB3m5RnJfn9ACTEXjNx5.jpeg)

**Cavarodes - Ostrea Virgula 2020**
Classic distinct Savagnin smell of orchard fruit, sesame leafs and acacia. First taste is lemon, salt, and a faint oxidative touch. Long finish and simply big. This is day two and it’s drinking so well. Structured and elegant. Will keep stocking up, if I can find at good prices…

**Michel Gahier - Macvin**
When it comes to fortified wines like this I believe it becomes difficult to tell what’s up and down in terms of flavor notes from one to another, but I feel like purity and texture is much more distinctive from winemaker to winemaker. In terms of this Macvin what’s more striking than others is the balance. Tissot is traditional, Cavarodes is a special kind of distinctive, Overnoy is powerful and so on and so on. That’s what I’d remember about this— balanced. A rebuy for sure.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_8688|375x500](upload://5AuFwvvvzIUwj4uxQjwF9UZdMzO.jpeg)


**Labet Varrons Pinot Noir 2022**

First thing that struck me was the clarity of the wine. Normally the Pinots of Labet has always been cloudy and very much a poster child of the low intervention qualities in good and bad, but this was very much the opposite of what I expected. Since last year they added the disclaimer of sensitivity to air, so I naturally opened with a bit of hesitation, but I think it wasn’t warranted here.

With first sniff it’s very apparent that carbonic maceration has been the method. Banana, mango and then very ripe and sweet strawberries take the stage. Tannins are basically undetectable. Tiny amount of five spice mix and earthiness. The table loved it. Me? For the 55€ going price these days then let me have a Foillard CdP next time for half the price. But it’s a beautiful glass.

**Daniel Sage Prosperite de l’Enterprise**

This screamed Pinot Noir at me more than the Labet, but texturally and tastewise it’s clearly Carignan and Merlot. Leather, plums and cherries. The carignan lends an amazing minerality and lightness to the ‘plummy’ character to the Merlot, which I actually think is sourced from Le Mazel(Oustric). Really nice bottle for the 25€ paid. Going to source more Sage for the dinner parties.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_8741|375x500](upload://g1DWVcbTfGEfPKkZ90QMJCQtOBj.jpeg)

**Cavarodes Poulsard Les Lumachelles 2020**
Really amazing straight out of the bottle with rhubarb, blood orange and a simple spiced vibe to it, like cinnamon and a bit of forrest floor. Classic dusty Poulsard color without being completely unclear, like the Houillon Bruyere consumed last weekend. Moderate acidity and berries for days. This is more alike the Overnoys I've had in the past. Wish I purchased more because this will age quite fine.

**Francois Dumas Saint Joseph 2016**
@LasseK was so kind to gift me this last year. Thanks!
Kind of a clear continuation of the Poulsard to this. This is not a classic Saint Joseph profile to me, as it's much more fruit forward. Blueberries and blackberries helped along by a generous acidity with a little classic meatiness and spice in the finish. Really elegant stuff. A few more decades to this I think! It's become a little hard to get access to though…

posts.name: Edward Thompson
posts.raw:
![IMG_3219|412x500](upload://mi7rdhw6dnnNHjvFxyqOV36k9O4.jpeg)
Gut Oggau is a producer I’ve had mixed experiences with. A few good ones, a few not good ones, and many that I thought fell flat relative to the price point. More hype than substance. 

Winifred 2023 is quite the delight, however! Beautiful salmon color. Vibrant acidity with a pleasant hint of VA. Lots of rose petal and berry on the nose alongside a bit of yeasty Bretty funk, much like a Belgian kriek. Thankfully, I didn’t get that Brett on the palate. Tart cranberry, tropical fruits, lots of florals, a hint of star anise like spice. This is dangerously gulpable. I paid near $50 for this I think, and truthfully for that I do expect a little more depth or nuance, but at the same time it was profoundly delicious and fun, so can’t complain.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.raw:
I think I have a huge amount of "natural wines" without knowing, just like in the past I had copious amounts of organic wines before actually knowing they were! I had this last night, which I'm guessing qualifies:

**Blanc des Côteaux - Cuvée Amphore - Domaine Thymiopoulos - Greece**

Not overly expressive nose of dried peach, honey and apricots, but much more impressive on the palate: a fascinating blend of honeycomb, yellow peach, and pineapple, with coriander and perhaps chamomile, then a lingering but crisp lemony finish. Great depth and complexity, quite rich, perhaps a little oxidative midpalate but it's not a problem, just delicious, so much so that we drank it far too quickly. Beautifully balanced, helped by the mere 13% ABV.
Easily the best Greek wine I've had, this was a Christmas present so I don't know the price, but I'm guessing around 30€ , perhaps more - anyway, I'd pay that anytime.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_8833|375x500](upload://fXKjmtFRyecKgZlqUFKgc0iXf8G.jpeg)


**Clos des Breteches Les Belouines 2021**

* The first night the nose was very dominated by the reductive notes of sesame seeds, cabbage and hay. Second night here all is resolved, with a very harmonic mouth and viscosity. Gentle honey notes along with quince and pear. I think this is a really nice Chenin Blanc. It has plenty of acidity to keep it lying for a while. After a few years where the second market price climbed to 90€ a bottle it seems it’s settled somewhere around the 45€ mark. Planning to open a few Chenin Blancs the next nights, so I’ll make my mind up then about what I prefer.

**Adrien Berlioz Le Grande Zeph 2020**

* Very shy that has just a touch of banana and pear. To be expected here. Long drink of tropical fruits and a lot of body. Quite like it but I’m worried how long it’d keep. Hmm

**Claire Naudin Orchis Mascula 2020**

* On opening night somewhat the best, and always has been with Claires wines. Funny that despite how clean and precise they seem.
The nose opens with purple fruits and amaretto intensified by a whiff of VA. Dark red fruits, stems and herbaciousness. As I just finished my ‘17s and they where in a good spot, I think I’ll take a breather on these ones.

**Maison Glandien, Les Ecully 2022**
Restaurant list find.

Very velvety Pinot Noir with plenty of fruit and life. Low tannins and and moderate acidity — more in a gamay'ish expression than strong burgundy. Ripe strawberries and a subtle layer of spice like clove. Quite like it! But those auction prices are a little crazy.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
New producer in Burgundy that was recommended by a merchant and this definitely overdelivered! ![IMG_1170|375x500](upload://hdt7QRIBZMvVOGBactqX0bymyu5.jpeg)
  • 2020 François Morente Volnay Les Poisets - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Volnay (4/23/2025)
    Earthy nose with red and dark cherries, underwood, lavender, and violets. Nice acidity with good structure, fruit driven palate although darker leaning flavors, black cherries, and blood orange zest on the palate. Good finish. This is quite impressive! A serious wine with plenty of depth and intensity that should age effortlessly. It's approachable now, but I get the feeling it's clearly holding back. I'd hold for 7+ years. 93+ (93 points)

This was a quite full on amber wine, very intense in its youth. Now 10 years from the vintage this is a less in your face amber wine and instead what stands out is the minerality, salinity and a more balanced wine (still fruits and herbal notes there).

posts.name: Edward Thompson
posts.raw:
![IMG_3253|375x500](upload://hYYIjqtDv24rK71fhuDFsXUeAeh.jpeg)
Trossen Rot Trocken 2019
Blend of Pinot Noir and Dornfelder, this one was quite unlike anything I’ve had before. At 10.5% I was expecting a very light and crunchy red, but that was a false presumption. Relatively dark plum color, lots of woodsy spice on the nose and palate. Going by the nose only and given the choice between this being a Mosel blend or a Bordeaux, I’d have chosen the latter. Yet the structure retains the light, acidic vibrancy you’d expect from a 10.5% natural wine. Delightfully gulpable and food friendly, but with a balance of flavors I wasn’t expecting. I checked and I paid $30 pre-tax for this, which I consider absolutely outstanding value.

posts.name: Victor About
posts.raw:
Hi @MadsW, Les Ecully is not a Pinot noir but a Gamay. Here's the description below: 

From ancient Gamay vineyards in the Beaujolais, grown on sandy soils. Produced using semi-carbonic maceration to highlight its juicy, red-fruit character, while maintaining freshness and complexity.

I had the 2023 and also found it good but not worth the secondary market prices for sure. 

Interesting fact, the 2023 is the last vintage during which Tino will produce Gamay with maison Glandien. So this cuvée won't exist anymore. Cheers.

posts.name: Victor About
posts.raw:
![IMG_8206|500x500](upload://Awyjve6MUZfH78KffMSyinP3KQ.jpeg)

First post has to be my favorite producer.

Saint Romain 2021 by Renaud Boyer. 

Pure, fresh, vibrant, and gorgeous Pinot Noir from Saint Romain in Burgundy, France. The color is a lovely translucent ruby. Expressive nose with notes of cherry, crushed strawberry, forest floor, mushroom, and cedar. Tart on the palate with bright acidity and a silky texture. This wine was so easily drinkable. Crushed strawberries, cranberries, and a hint of raspberries and minerals. Medium length finish. 

I always preferred Renaud’s whites over the reds, but this wine may have changed my mind. They are both superb. What I’m most impressed about is that Renaud was able to produce such a beautiful red from the 2021 vintage.

2011 Milos Plavac

Mainly ungrafted Plavac Mali, grown on an amphitheater like vineyard next to the Dalmatian sea on Peljesac and a contender for one of the most beautiful vineyards. And best QPR wine around. 

This entry bottling shows no sign whatsoever of loosing steam. Dark and red fruits, herbal, hint of Brett. Still bite to the tannins, good freshness with cutting acidity, salinity and a very long finish. 

Day two and three it just gets better.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
Great minds think alike @LasseK 
Also thought I'd do a review now.

Sooo, I'm heading back to Korea on Sunday, so time for a final fun weekend.

**Georges Comte - Les Pelerins 2014**
* Nose is dominated by lemon juice and yoghurt. Long drink with laser acidity leaning into an elegant and mouth filling sip of salty lemon and a touch of honey. Very linear. Can cellar for two more decades easy. 
On day 2 no particular development.

**Ferme de la Sansonniere - La Lune 2017**
* Deep honey notes in the nose and a long candied peach vibe. The amphore version in comparison to this had no RS. Here it’s 8mg and I already find it disturbing. I don’t really get the variation from Mark Angeli concerning these wines? It’s flabby.

**Claire Naudin Myosotis Arvensis 2021**
* Strong nose of pepper, cherry and a whiff of tobacco. Really good acidity keeping it really fresh and with the stemminess of it, it’s a very upright and broadshouldered wine. Big. Curious to see where it lands in 3 years time. The rest of the case will sleep til ’28

**Domaine Labet - Assemblage 2018**
* Second time with this since August. Big big big sous voile. Very fragrant cinnamon, incense, curry. I mean it’s to me the best miniature VJ out there, and I’d almost rather do this one at half the price. 
On day 2 this has become even better.

**Domaine Labet - Macvin**
* This macvin from Labet is among my favorites. Cloves, cinnamon, vanilla and well, yes, raisins. Lovely little streak of VA or acidity that keeps it really fun and fresh.  So good. 

![IMG_9016|375x500](upload://yYvs4NYM0fhj99C5sMBWhnTGpmO.jpeg)

2016 CASAS DE ENRIBA DATERRA VITICULTORES (LAURA LORENZO)

Closed and quite “smokey” the first night, put aside and on day two this is a lot more rewarding. Sampled over 3-4 days. 

Red fruits and berries (strawberries),quite some herbal notes, spices, floral.

Sneaky tannins, high acidity and a long finish that reinforces all elements of the wine. Showing really well, except it will keep going for quite some time, perhaps alap improve. Quite impressive.

2019 La Vigne des Pères (Aymeric Paillard) St. Joseph Petit Pere
(and home baked bread)

![IMG_0376|666x500](upload://lc0MFTaatVE4oCN3fx8NQNXPAXd.jpeg)

More rough work than fun at this stage. Ripping tannins, a touch of brett, screeching acid, a bit of va. Very dark, a typical Northern Rhone Syrah mix: dark fruit, black olives, leather, meat, tar. Calming down a bit on day 2 and 3. Challenging, not uninteresting, but not easy to handle without food.

posts.name: Victor About
posts.raw:
![496641793_18059283683330528_7734768191422386273_n|500x500](upload://fn0CxNaOmHmIFheDa1J471QcBnV.jpeg)


I've also had mixed experiences with Mark Angeli's wines in the past. Especially with Les Blanderies. But I've enjoyed the most recent vintages of La Lune (21 & 22). I think the real problem is that these wines have gotten a bit too expensive. So here's a Chenin that really impressed me and that I consider great value for money (Paid 32 euros for it). I had Batardiere's L'Esprit Libre the other day and this was much more impressive and serious for only 2 more euros. 

Les Enfers Tranquilles 2021 by Michel Autran.

Fresh, rich, and tense Chenin from Vouvray, France. The color is medium to pale gold. Expressive nose with notes of Bruised apples, lemon, vanilla, a hint of honey, and butter. Crisp acidity on the palate. Pear, citrus, cream, and minerals. Medium length finish.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
Final wines from here. Now back to the ocassional bottle.

![IMG_9020|375x500](upload://dxpDV5Luk0zuqYDcCvyNq0yNVEz.jpeg)



**Stephane Bernaudeau - Coqueries 2022**

The nose doesn’t do much besides a very elegant red apple scent and a bit of reduction. The acidity is on the strong side and it’s of course a bit young all things considered, but there’s a big wine beneath.
The mouth is dominated by white orchard fruits but centered on ripe red apple.

As another note on CT points out, there’s a slight mousiness to the wine. Not enough to disturb me, but I wouldn’t have expected this. Notes from previous vintages doesn’t mention this, so must be an anomaly.

**Mémée Marie - Francois Rousset Martin 2017**

Really amazing nose of caramel, lime and a bit of hay. Very classic Jura profile. Suppossedly made as the ‘entry’ cuvee for FRM, but after having had many wines of his, this really can stand with the ‘better’ ones.
I think it’s 95% Chardonnay and 5% Savagnin, which lends a powerful hand, especially on the nose. There’s a slight oxidative character, which I find interesting as a ‘welcome to Jura’ kind of entry wine. Great stuff. Where did those days go when it was 22€ a bottle?

**La Fleur au Fusil - Vins du Cabanon(Castex) 2022**

This jumped right out the glass with ripe red berries and ‘spiciness’. So lively and fun. Not particularly deep but the fruit that is the center and front is very polished for a producer as wild as Castex was. I really liked it.
posts.cooked:
Final wines from here. Now back to the ocassional bottle.

IMG_90201920×2560 363 KB

Stephane Bernaudeau - Coqueries 2022

The nose doesn’t do much besides a very elegant red apple scent and a bit of reduction. The acidity is on the strong side and it’s of course a bit young all things considered, but there’s a big wine beneath.
The mouth is dominated by white orchard fruits but centered on ripe red apple.

As another note on CT points out, there’s a slight mousiness to the wine. Not enough to disturb me, but I wouldn’t have expected this. Notes from previous vintages doesn’t mention this, so must be an anomaly.

La Fleur au Fusil - Vins du Cabanon(Castex) 2022

This jumped right out the glass with ripe red berries and ‘spiciness’. So lively and fun. Not particularly deep but the fruit that is the center and front is very polished for a producer as wild as Castex was. I really liked it.

![Capture3|374x500, 100%](upload://3XaNuZoVZFJej5LM26gMxohkl2r.jpeg) Henri et Gilles Buisson Bourgogne Rouge. Organic, biodynamic. Yet another fantastic wine from Rosenthal. Just can't seem to miss with him. For $30, this is fantastic.
  • 2022 Henri et Gilles Buisson Bourgogne - France, Burgundy, Bourgogne (5/11/2025)
    Popped and poured. Bottle has no capsule or wax, perhaps indicating that this is meant to be drank young.

    Pours a light scintillating ruby-red. The nose starts a bit reductive but then blossoms into ripe cherry, forest berry, and a lovely smoke. The rustic fruit is so lively as to be mistaken for a Beaujolais. The palate is a fine blend of fruit and savory notes that follow the palate, with riverbank stony minerality and light spice. The structure is perfect right now -- there is balanced acidity and just a brush of integrated tannins.

    One of the finer Bourgogne Rouges I have tasted recently. Everything is just so well put together, glou glou with some nuance and complexity. (90 points)

Tried Francois Rousset-Martin for the first time recently, notes below.

2020 Francois Rousset-Martin ‘Cuvee du Professeur’ Savagnin 

Jura, France

100% Savagnin from the legendary marl soils of Château-Chalon, vinified ouillé (topped-up in the Burgundy style). It’s incredibly fresh, yet shows just a touch of sherry-like oxidative character. This is more on the Burgundian or traditional side of Jura as opposed to the wild side.

The nose is wild and expressive, think stinky cheese, cola, brioche, lemon curd, apricot, and a hint of reduction. On the palate, it’s electric: high-acid, taut, and layered with ripe tropical fruit, sweet almond, stone fruit, and that subtle oxidative edge that just enhances the whole package.

Now I understand why this wine has such a following. Totally captivating.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
**Bruno Schueller - Grand Cru Eichberg Riesling 2003**

* My wife hit a milestone with work so I decided to open a fun one for her. 
 Nose of acacia honey, ripe honeymelon, ginger, and finally a whiff of mushrooms, as we are in the Alsace after all. The color is golden, and the residual sugar is playing well with the acidity at this age. The finish is long. It’s at the height or a little beyond my preferred drinking window, but it’s a fantastic Riesling from Schueller. If you look past my preferences in terms of age, then this can do another few years. Harmonic and beautiful riesling. Love it. 

*Edit: On day two I'm realising that upon opening it was disjointed. The sugar and acid was somewhat two different components, although in their own rights well composed, but today they're fused. How I didn't see that. That's why we keep bottles for a few days…

For the hunters of Brunos wines out there, then after a number of wines provided by a solid source, I can say that the Bildstoeckle Riesling showed the best after these years. This Eichberg and Pfersigberg '99 differed by having a tad bit more ripeness in store, but was showing a little more advanced.

![IMG_9126|375x500](upload://z9DlboCvg2AD4K8L5KoarpeRSlE.jpeg)
![IMG_9128|375x500](upload://qFeY8ufGuovlx86KNJEEUgpJrbl.jpeg)

    Delicious. Dense berry fruit, iron, violet florals, hint of brett, and fine tannins. You can taste some carbonic on the finish after a couple hours open, but this really seems to be a wine that ages out of it, i wish i had more.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.raw:
• 2022 Maison Maenad Savagnin Côtes du Jura Reveil Spectral - France, Jura, Côtes du Jura (5/24/2025)
    This has a very attractive, lifted nose of lemon peels and herbs with a slight tropical note. The wine itself is bottled electricity: absolutely razor sharp acidity that is mouth-watering and precise, buoying the wine's airy weight and delicate mouthfeel. Sharp salinity, citrus, crushed rocks and slightly ripe yellow fruit define the palate. While these flavors are not overly complex or layered at this point (it doesn't quite have the same tension and ripeness of fruit that you get from Ganevat, whom the winemaker trained under), it is just so sappy and moreish, you cannot help but want to keep drinking after the first sip. With some time in the glass, a slightly creamy texture develops, but does not muddle the wine's linear focus or its rather long finish. Rather, the wine keeps improving throughout the course of the evening. A very satisfying and exciting wine from an up-and-coming producer.

    The '22 Reveil Spectral is Maison Maenad's first vintage of Savagnin from the lieu-dit of Au Carre. It's very different from the '20 L'Avant (the only other bottle of Maison Maenad I've ever had) which is a Chardonnay from rented vines in Les Varrons, which seemed more understated, more elegant, and more cerebral, but less racy and in-your-face. Perhaps the L'Avant's finer, lacier character may be a product of terroir (or grape variety and vintage) or the winemaker needing to dial in this particular cuvee, but part of me really appreciates the intensity of the Reveil Spectral. (The '22 L'Avant, which I didn't have, will be the last vintage of that cuvee).

    Elevage: 2 years in 500 ml barrels.

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 2022 Domaine Antoine Lienhardt Côte de Nuits Villages Emphase - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Côte de Nuits Villages (5/24/2025)
    A beautifully expressive and complex nose of wild strawberries, flowers, and subtle green and earthy notes that's very evocative of whole clusters. The palate has a silky texture, seamless acidity, and elegant weight. Its incredibly sweet dark red fruit is layered with complex earthy and green notes of sous bois, bitter herbs, and a smoky minerality that crescendos on the finish. This is really superb for the first hour, before the wine nosedives with air and its clarity of flavors loses focus and becomes muddled. It is still drinkable, but it was quite surprising to have a wine start so superbly on pop and pour, and then to decline so rapidly with time in the glass. I still like it, but I'm skeptical about the ageability of this wine.

    Tech: Fruit selected from Lienhardt's several parcels of 60+ years-old vines in the village of Comblanchien. Semi-carbonic whole cluster maceration in tank before indigenous yeast fermentation. Elevage is 12 months in oak barrels (10% new).

Posted from CellarTracker

![IMG_9151|349x500](upload://7nwPFs9jlL2YmkXPbAB7gVE1I7O.jpeg)

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.raw:
![1000002690|375x500](upload://9KiBguFcIB25I1gSskOiu8xeIji.jpeg)

Pretty good Chenin.

High slightly volatile acidic nature. Everything apples and lemon. Very reductive. Notes of salt and sesame. A hint of some baking spices.

It doesn't scream Chenin. Would probably have guessed Jura Chardonnay blind. If you can handle the acidic nature of it, it is a nice wine. No traces of any flaws.

I would have liked it to have a bit more fruit and body to play against the acidity, but still enjoying it a lot and happy to have two more.
