posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
We had a little Auvergne tasting ("Beauger and friends") yesterday. This is really such an interesting and unique region with excellent and quality driven producers. I definitely don't have or drink enough Auvergne wines...
  • 2015 Domaine Pierre Beauger Rotten Highway - France, Vin de France (6/8/2025)
    Tasted next to the 2016 RH. Really interesting nose with intense flavors of apricot, orange, stone fruit, some botrytis, and minerals. Nice acidity on the palate with ripe fruit, guava, oranges, and more minerals. Nice finish that lingers for a while. There's so much going on and it continuously changes in the glass. The wine is extremely ripe, but well balanced and completely dry. What a unique, singular expression of Sauvignon Blanc! (95 points)
  • 2016 Domaine Pierre Beauger Rotten Highway - France, Vin de France (6/8/2025)
    Tasted next to the 2015 RH. This was significantly funkier on the nose with notes of lemon, tropical fruit, brett, smoke, and a bit of reduction. Similar intensity and nice acidity on the palate with more structure and textural mouthfeel, apricot flavors, and a bit of citrus. Nice finish. Overall, this was very interesting but perhaps a little more weird compared to the 2015. Much lower alcohol (10.37% vs 14.4%) and quite a bit more tannic with noticeable brett. This may significantly benefit from a few years in the cellar. 92++ (92 points)
  • 2018 Domaine Pierre Beauger PN18 - France, Vin de France (6/8/2025)
    Lovely nose with a hint of reduction that blew off with air, followed by notes of cherries, roses, and pine needles. The nose feels very Burgundian and quite complex. Nice acidity on the palate with more cherries, red berries, blood orange, and tart fruit. Long finish. Beautiful wine that really blossomed with air. This should be very special in 5-10 years. 94-95+ (94 points)
  • 2018 L'Arbre Blanc (Frederic Gounan) Vin de France Les Grandes Orgues - France, Vin de France (6/8/2025)
    Very nice nose with blood orange, red cherries, and tart fruit. High acidity on the palate with flavors of red cherries, raspberries, white pepper, and berries. Nice finish. This is a lovely wine that needs time to fully shine. At the moment it's very primary and sharp with tart but balanced flavors. I'd give it 5-7+ years to fully come together. 93-94++ (93 points)
  • 2018 Mito Inoue Flow - France, Loire Valley, Côtes d'Auvergne (6/8/2025)
    Really pretty nose with elegant notes of cherries, flowers, red berries, and just a tiny hint of brett. Great acidity on the palate with more tart cherries, strawberries, and forest floor notes. Long finish. This was super good. What an interesting, elegant, and unique expression of Gamay! (95 points)

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_9271|375x500](upload://pmNqN5KDxQK2kE7qqdQdYVkfq1k.jpeg)

* **Domaine de la Tournelle - Savagnin Voile 2016**

Upon opening, the bottle was lightning captured in a bottle. It needed half a day to open up somewhat and then it became approachable. Compared to other similar cuvées, this stands apart by its fruit, which to me is very positive and really distinct. Most other sous voile wines, and here I mean sous voile as in 5 years of voile and not the 7 like a classic VJ, are to me always very heavy sotolon forward things of curry and spice with fruit almost on a secondary note. As for the mouth, this is very green apple dominant with a piercing acidity and a long finish. There’s a slight sweetness to the finish, which is peach-like, but I could be a little off.	
 
What I find so nice about this cuvee is that normally, to me, the other SV wines can be very divided — Sotolon — Fruit — Acidity. However, this cuvée is very harmonic in that everything is joined from the first sip. It’s a very long time ago that I had the VJ, but that too was very beautiful. Paid some 45€, and that's perfectly fine.
posts.cooked:
IMG_92711920×2560 190 KB

  • Domaine de la Tournelle - Savagnin Voile 2016

Upon opening, the bottle was lightning captured in a bottle. It needed half a day to open up somewhat and then it became approachable. Compared to other similar cuvées, this stands apart by its fruit, which to me is very positive and really distinct. Most other sous voile wines, and here I mean sous voile as in 5 years of voile and not the 7 like a classic VJ, are to me always very heavy sotolon forward things of curry and spice with fruit almost on a secondary note. As for the mouth, this is very green apple dominant with a piercing acidity and a long finish. There’s a slight sweetness to the finish, which is peach-like, but I could be a little off.

What I find so nice about this cuvee is that normally, to me, the other SV wines can be very divided — Sotolon — Fruit — Acidity. However, this cuvée is very harmonic in that everything is joined from the first sip. It’s a very long time ago that I had the VJ, but that too was very beautiful. Paid some 45€, and that’s perfectly fine.

For all the americans on WB im surprised there aren't more american bottles of natural wine being posted in this thread! I opened a bottle of Stagiare chardonnay over the weekend and was very impressed by it, lots of lemon character with a touch of salinity+oxidation to give it some more oomph. Definitely felt that the jura was a reference point, but with riper california fruit. 0/0 still wine bottled under crown cap (unclear why, maybe cheaper+easier than cork?), I would definitely recommend this producer if you see them on a list somewhere - i think he changes the cuvee names every year. 
![IMG_4786|375x500](upload://4ClVXQ5D0I6NHjZ4k8oUSD3nony.jpeg)

posts.name: Rahsaan Maxwell
posts.raw: I'm not a regular poster in this thread, but thought folks might have views on the 2020 Jakob Tennstedt Mauer Fuchs Riesling. I know better than to try this in the US, where the bottles seem to be regularly beaten up. But figured when in Germany, why not get a taste. And those tastes were delicious! Needs some air to shake off the initial natty funk, but then it becomes a very nice wine. Of course plenty of hazy nutty sour 'natural' wine notes, but also some nice firm structured red apple skin texture, plus juicy berry notes, and crystalline cut on the finish to complement the cloudy natural haze. I would happily order this while lounging in a wine bar. Which is perhaps the intended demographic. But, I have no plans to purchase more, because I would be hard pressed to identify this as a riesling, or as a Mosel wine, or even as a German wine. It could be from anywhere. Still, while not for me to chase, I'm sure others enjoy... ![6346|375x500, 75%](upload://hgOTFdZqXW3IjHM5KBTWH3t0g8K.jpeg) ![6349|375x500, 75%](upload://kr94hTAZyiwrNTtinx0RqNySJtA.jpeg)

posts.name: Marco Manieri
posts.raw:
![IMG_3818|415x500](upload://nVQLNA1wwUX5kPVr760TXYoAjUM.jpeg)

First time with Rossese and this producer. More evolution than I’d expect, fruit was lacking. Otherwise nicely balanced with a dry finish. Maybe a slightly defective cork that let some oxygen in? 

Also first time with Lano. A little odd version of Nebbiolo with blueish color and blackberry on the nose. Nice acidity but very dominant greenish tannins that didn’t integrate very well despite fairly short maceration (12-15 days) and a year in tonneaux. My guesses were all over the place, could not pinpoint Nebbiolo at all. 

Le Trame from Giovanna Morganti was pure Sangiovese seduction with immaculate balance, structure and elegance.This 2016 is in a good place now. P’n’p, it didn’t hold it together when I revisited the bottle after two days

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.raw:
A tasting of 2023 Les Horees reds with family in the Charente.

No serious real notes as we just enjoyed.

Coteaux - light elegant spiced raspberries, firm acidity, very easy drinking

Six Ifs - prettier style, fragrant, yet more robust in the mouth.

Pernand Verglesses 1er Fichots - a bit more reserve here, slightly darker fruits, still that refreshing acidity. And so elegant.

Beaune Prevoilles - very expressive Beaune, cherry fruits and spice, more complex and concentrated.

Beaune 1 er Toussaints - beautiful nose, elegant perfumed, carries more heft, supple, a bit chewy even, ripe in the mouth. Serious. Seriously good.

Gevrey - reductive, more spice, still light and elegant, bright cherry, very pure, aniseed, firm, supple tannins.

![IMG_0003|375x500](upload://ySo4A0WTxN6Ei6QaKOy0n1SZ0tM.jpeg)




Disclaimer - I was given these open bottles by Catharina (whose wines we sell) this morning. Right place at the right time as I collected a few bottles for the shop.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.raw:
![1000003247|375x500](upload://A2SIl0BKzJ1ptWclPcipfUJPIi.jpeg)

A great set of wines

Labet (2022) was all the lemon salty savagnin tension that I crave. Young, but Labet is awesome young.

First time trying a wine from Marthe Henry. A lovely light Burgundy with candied berries and some lovely floral, herbal and cinnamon notes. Nothing volatile. A rebuy.

Marnes Blanches 2019 En Quatre Vis has always been a stellar wine, and easily followed along with the more expensive bottles. Such a lovely butter fudge note with some melon, apples and pinapple. But still with the expected acidic tension and some lemon and salty notes. Very happy to have a few more left.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_9472|374x500](upload://mm3eAwUHbSkH1SelWCoATw7HUIs.jpeg)

**Chateau de Beru - Chablis, 2021**

Yellow flowers, citrus and a tiny touch of vanilla. This is the common theme in both nose and mouth. Maybe there’s a touch more oak than I want to see, but it’s a very nice and tense acidic profile otherwise that gives me a sense of the house style, I guess. Would not be scared to order one off a list in Europe, but it’s not what I want to stock my cellar with.

**Tempier Bandol Rosé 2018**
Light salmon towards deep onion peel color. Peaches, slight grapefruit and dried herbs. The balance is quite nice, a touch of bitterness helps the freshness as the acidity is a tad below what I'm expecting. The expected texture is there with some chewiness. Good rosé and I don't see an upside to cellaring this vintage any longer.

![20250802_185236|375x500](upload://ifXnqR4fhnrjIPPT4cfVp2IiA23.jpeg)

**Anne et JF Ganevat Arces 2018**
100% Chard, ouillé. The last time I had this was in 2021 and it was closed. Same thing upon opening but it only took about 15 minutes in the glass to open up. The dry extract was there from the get go but it changed from pure lemon to ripe citrus, apple pie and quince. Bright acidity all the way through but the texture is chewy and brings a lot of complexity to the wine. Decent finish. Good wine and still some upside in cellaring.
posts.cooked:
Not sure Tempier really hits the theme but Ganevat (négoce) does.

20250802_1733541920×2560 256 KB

Tempier Bandol Rosé 2018
Light salmon towards deep onion peel color. Peaches, slight grapefruit and dried herbs. The balance is quite nice, a touch of bitterness helps the freshness as the acidity is a tad below what I’m expecting. The expected texture is there with some chewiness. Good rosé and I don’t see an upside to cellaring this vintage any longer.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.raw:
![20250804_112530|375x500](upload://p0rtDqDJErfnbQgoQaLQn8IoMGe.jpeg)
**Fitapreta O Ancestral 2020**
Cellartracker said I consumed 3 before leaving for France yet I have no recollection of it. Looking into it further, I gifted the 3 bottles, including one to my friend @Peter_Chiu. So this was my first go at it.

Roupeiro, Rabo de Ovelha, Antão Vaz, Tamarez, Alicante Branco and Arinto. Old vines (going back to 1970). Around 30,000 bottles produced. Ripe citrus, dried apricots, hints of honeycomb and a little sea breeze going on. Good texture on the palate, concentrated with proper medium acidity and a long finish on salted hazelnuts. This punches above its pay grade. Glad I have a few remaining.

![20250804_125732|375x500](upload://mTR26mWlCZxLGgBMMLyrCdKiEzt.jpeg)
**Pranzegg Vino Rosso Leggero 2022**
Schiava (mostly, I think) and Lagrein. 11.5%
The crown cap gives it away, this is as glou-glou as they come. Better served chilled, it's all fresh red berries and summer florals. No spritziness, thank god, and enjoyable on a warm day with charcuterie and crudités. Fun.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
![IMG_1850|375x500](upload://oFMdOzF8JrXbhSV5FfqucwBOOgq.jpeg) Really enjoyed these two natural wines with Chinese food this past weekend...
  • 2011 Maison Valette Pouilly-Fuissé Clos Reyssié - France, Burgundy, Mâconnais, Pouilly-Fuissé (8/3/2025)
    Golden color. Super interesting nose with lemon, apple, some oxidative notes, almonds, a bit of VA, citrus, minerals, and a distinct ginger note that emerged with air. Nice acidity and balance on the palate with more citrus flavors, a bit of sous voile feel, lemon, crushed rocks, and a hint more of the VA. Long finish. This was a great experience! Complex and very interesting with lots of depth, although not for everyone... (94 points)
  • 2021 Daniel Sage Nyctalopie - France, Vin de France (8/3/2025)
    Very nice and fragrant nose with floral notes, a bit of brett, earth, and a hint of VA. Light palate with nice acidity, flavors of strawberries, red cherries, blood orange, and a bit more underwood. Nice finish. This is a lovely, light, and lively natural wine. It's ready to drink and goes down way too easily, but it has good complexity and should reward short term aging. (93 points)

What bottle of wine did you open today? (Part 2) WINE TALK
[IMG_9561] Domaine L’Anglore, Tavel Vintage 2020 Black pepper, cinnamon and clove. Red ripe fruits on a hot scorching dusty day. There’s tannins and a bit of licorice and smoke going on in the finish. Nice acidity that will allow for some mid-term aging but i’m not sure that I’d find that more pleasurable than what it is at this point. Very nice and beautiful glass. For the 35€ I paid, lower than a recent Pataille Fleur de Pinot, I find this a much deeper and focused glass. It’s a rebuy.

![IMG_1960|375x500](upload://p8oFaPWLsoF2R1pu6UMdt2A3zoW.jpeg)

Three lovely natural wines from a few days ago.

The Bornard was quite impressive. All the 15s and 16s I've had (still made by the father) have been really good...

Carteret was very good, quite young, and primary. It's an easy drinking and somewhat simple wine but super enjoyable. The Euphoria, however, is a huge step up.

The 3.14 was great, too. I wasn't that impressed with the 17s when released, but really enjoyed the last two bottles. This can age for a decade or two longer.

Wasenhaus cheaper and fun glou glou wine.
100% whole cluster and some carbonic maceration.

I had honestly forgotten about this bottle.

But after two years in bottle it have evolved beautifully! The nose is full on wholecluster spice and a fruity from the carbonic. So aromatic! The taste is fun and simple, but for a warm summer day it was perfect.

Think I paid 16€...

A unicorn wine to me, but I’m also not exactly willing to pay that.

Sylvie's Wine Auctions

La Star (Pierre Weyand)

Eric Callcut was a legendary but short-lived winemaker. He worked his plots from 1995 to 1999 and then sold some vineyards to Stéphane Bernardeau. Including the mythical plots of 'les Onglés' and 'les Nourrissons'.

posts.name: Victor About
posts.raw: I tried his blanc liqoreux once, "Le Bouquet du Paradis des Bonnes Blanches". It was one of the best, if not the best, sweet wine I've ever tried. So much depth and a unique slightly viscous texture. I loved it but I would never pay the prices on Idealwine for it. Sadly Pierre Weyand died in a tractor accident and there's a lot of speculation surrounding his wines nowadays.

**2015 Passionate Wine Via Revolucionaria Torrontés Brutal**

Intoxicating aromatics that I would situate into the Gewürztraminer meets Moscato de Alexandria territory. The palate is bone dry, dominated by a kumquat-like citrus profile, a touch o dry grapefruit peel, a but of yeast funk and a very fine tannic grip. Complex and refreshing at the same time.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_9604|375x500](upload://sWBCx5qFzC0HMh5sFoPRmj3O1uk.jpeg)

Lou Dumont - Chorey-les-Beaune 2022

I guess Lou Dumont belongs in here. Final good bottle before three weeks in Europe, starting Monday. Highlight of going this time will hopefully be picking grapes at Overnoy/Houillon in Jura, but otherwise nearly two weeks in Paris and then a few days at my cellar. 

Ah back to the wine… Drop dead gorgeous nose of cinnamon, chocolate and ripe red fruits. Great sip of ripe cherries and strawberries with a solid acidity to hold up against the ripeness. Persistent tannins. It can be saved for aging a handful of years but it's pretty nice now. No-brainer for the price in europe, if you can find it.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
I've been traveling in Europe for the past month and didn't check the forum that frequently... Here are some of the highlights of the past four weeks: ![IMG_2116|375x500](upload://lJBmP3P7vUvMBycw8hSvxLZBeNG.jpeg)
  • 2015 Les Jardins De La Martinière X - France, Loire Valley (8/22/2025)
    Awesome nose with very complex notes of lemon, apple, a bit of oxidation, dried herbs, honey, a hint of VA, and a bit of riper stone fruit that emerged with air. Great acidity on the palate with textural mouthfeel, flavors of orange zest, lemon, chamomile, minerals, and green apple. Long finish. Wow, this is an amazing wine! Complex and deep, it kept changing in the glass to reveal layer after layer. 96-97+ (97 points)
![IMG_2148|375x500](upload://co7RZKFlFm3delgapx3c4xYMivO.jpeg)
  • 2020 Emmanuel Houillon (Maison Pierre Overnoy) Arbois Pupillin Poulsard / Trousseau - France, Jura, Arbois Pupillin (8/25/2025)
    Very pretty nose with notes of strawberries, red cherries, a bit of flowers, and blood orange. Good acidity on the palate with a hint of tannic structure, more red berries, wild strawberries, forest notes, violets, and bright fruit. Nice finish. Really lovely wine that is young and enjoyable now, but I expect this to age just as well as the regular Poulsard. It may not be profound yet, but could develop into something special. 94++ (94 points)
![IMG_2180|375x500](upload://rWRb6RXqAq7UWCG4SpKEUYMNel9.jpeg)
  • 2004 Maison Valette Pouilly-Fuissé Tradition - France, Burgundy, Mâconnais, Pouilly-Fuissé (8/28/2025)
    Beautiful nose with lemon, almonds, hay, minerals, and hazelnuts notes. Pretty high acidity on the palate with more lemon flavors, salinity, green apple, more nuts, and dried flowers. Long finish. The wine is clearly mature without oxidation and still retains freshness and fruit. Fantastic experience and super enjoyable. (95 points)
![IMG_2316|375x500](upload://5dsNCKoUPxfZzJCvegqFl5qkh94.jpeg)
  • 2019 Mathieu Allante et Christian Boulanger Phenomaynal - France, Vin de France (9/3/2025)
    Gorgeous nose with complex notes of apples, lemon, minerals, a slightly peppery notes, and maybe a hint of almonds. High acidity on the palate with more lemon and mineral flavors, salinity, and citrus zest. Long finish. This was a fantastic showing! Very fresh and lively with a bit less VA compared to previous bottles and perhaps a bit more depth. This should only continue to improve... (96 points)

    Beautiful nose with lemon, almonds, hay, minerals, and hazelnuts notes. Pretty high acidity on the palate with more lemon flavors, salinity, green apple, more nuts, and dried flowers. Long finish. The wine is clearly mature without oxidation and still retains freshness and fruit. Fantastic experience and super enjoyable. (95 points)

IMG_23161920×2560 447 KB

  • 2019 Mathieu Allante et Christian Boulanger Phenomaynal - France, Vin de France (9/3/2025)
    Gorgeous nose with complex notes of apples, lemon, minerals, a slightly peppery notes, and maybe a hint of almonds. High acidity on the palate with more lemon and mineral flavors, salinity, and citrus zest. Long finish. This was a fantastic showing! Very fresh and lively with a bit less VA compared to previous bottles and perhaps a bit more depth. This should only continue to improve... (96 points)

posts.raw:
![IMG_4260|375x500](upload://jQwS7WahxKWV1FjDB23e00BJWnb.jpeg)
Lots of ripe crunchy fruit and then some rose-like florals and a bit of wood-influenced spice. Overall a little thin and lacking intensity. Both on the nose and palate, I found myself craving something... more. The only thing it had in spades was acidity, which walked a fine line between acetic VA and sweet-tart watermelon Sour Patch Kids. Not unpleasant, but the potency of the acid profile when juxtaposed with the rest of the wine yielded something lacking poise and balance.

For a bottle I had reasonably high expectations for, this one fell flat. I've many bottles of Pataille's rose and whites and always enjoyed them thoroughly, so I figured I'd probably go for this as well. It certainly wasn't bad, but it didn't delivery nearly enough in either character or poise to justify its $85 price tag. Blinded on this I would've pegged it as something like a sub-$20 entry level zero-zero Beaujolais. Again, nothing was actually *bad* here, simply disappointed.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_9830 (1)|375x500](upload://spLeeVxw4MWkEeD9rowawXE9Xbd.jpeg)


**Patrick Corbineau Presses 2018**
Very beautiful rustic Cab Franc from one of my favorite producers. Sous bois, healthy acidity, brett, blackcurrant. Just amazing, and it’ll last 15+ years. Sad he passed away this year.

**De Moor Nuova Descriptio 2022**
Very healthy yellow fruits from the SB, a bit of structure from the macerated Aligoté, and typicity from Chardonnay. Really a nice and vibrant glass. A bit of saltiness and Jurassic qualities to it.

**Charles Dufour – Cuisine du Dimanche**
As opposed to some of the previous iterations, this one is highly oxidative and more coppery in color. It has reserve blends from 1982 (?), so it’s really out there. I prefer the previous ones, but good QPR. Russell, I'm curious to read your notes if there's a mousiness to it or if it's the yeasty nutty quality of an acient reserve playing tricks on me. 

**Labet Varrons Chardonnay 2022**
For the young age, very complex and promising. Salty, good fruit, and beautiful viscosity. Kicking myself for not being able to smell this properly today. It's just such an elegant glass, even not being able to taste 80% of what's going on. Oh it's so good.

**Labet Poulsard en Billat 2023**
Just a fantastic and seductive wine. Light pale red color with good complexity and a kaleidoscope of taste and scent. Tomato consommé, rooibos tea, ripe strawberries. It of course doesn’t have the depth, ageability, and concentration of Overnoy, but it’s the “other side” of Poulsard. So velvety and dreamy. Oh my.

**Labet Vin Jaune 2015**
Such a deep and complex drink with so much energy. Curry, caramel, cinnamon, and cardamom. So much power and finesse in this VJ. To me stylistically it's very similar to the ones from Ganevat. Best VJ in a long time. So happy I bought a lot of this.

**Gahier Macvin**
As ever and always, Gahier just shines through. It’s conservative but always with an edge. The difference between this Macvin and the one from Ganevat is in the intensity: Ganevat, as always, is salty, fruity, and warm, while this is sharp, traditional, and tempered. Nice QPR.

**Jardin de la Martiniere - Ko-Ki**
Rustic Loire CF typicity similar to the Corbineau. Acidity a little higher than what you normally see at this kind of rendition. Really like it. Does it mirror the second market prices of +120€? No…

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_9804|375x500](upload://yg7IKLm4X2LoZZ8SsvUkb203yhe.jpeg)

**2022 Domaine Dominique Dufour Aussigouins Experto...R ...**
We had this, or a "lower cuvee", at the Overnoy headquarter a few days before and I remember everyone silently bopping their heads like 'yes' when they had their first sip. This was the same experience once it opened up after 40 minutes time. Beeswax, stone fruits and a tight lipsmacking acidity. It's perfectly balanced and it suggests it'll do well with 10 years of aging. Really good stuff. It sells at 50€ which is a little high, and I'm a little unsure about what we paid at the bar.

A little mention here also. Thanks @VictorA for being so genereous to spoil me with wine and cheese on my final day in Paris. I can only recommend giving him a follow on Instagram and see if he has time to meet. I'm surely looking forward to having a small tasting in February/March together.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.raw:
![1000003879|375x500](upload://wqAdhxQRZBICKXXgt16eHdgJmvo.jpeg)

Yellow fruits. It has developed notes of nuts and lightly bruised, mature autumn apples. It’s the lemon-heaven we all want from our Jura. Salty, mineral. A subtle hint of something raw, fennel-like, almost licorice. Spices from the sweet kitchen. A touch of oak? 

The acidity is high, but without the sharpness of newer vintages. The fruit is certainly there, but not as ripe and intense as in more recent years. Slighty more classic perhaps. 

However it is an excellent wine. At fifteen years it is drinking beautifully. Still so fresh, well-balanced, with great developed depth of flavor, and still with pure Jura DNA. 

This vintage comes from a transitional period at the domaine. Read this old article to get a better understanding of what was going on back then:

https://jurawine.co.uk/2013/02/21/domainelabetafamilyaffair/

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.raw:
Over the years, I've had a few Pierre Naigeon wines I really enjoyed (Fixin, Gevrey and Marsannay). They were sometimes a little austere but some vintages offered very good wines at more than decent prices.

Le Domaine Les Astrelles took over the Naigeon vines. We got a few of their lower end stuff in Quebec so I wanted to try them. 

I think their stuff is mostly parcellaire.

![20251005_170332|375x500](upload://aeU4r6UrrxbGoSfWNHiZNnjVdBm.jpeg)
**Les Astrelles Aligoté Les Larres 2022**
I was surprised this was only 10% in new barrel as the presence of wood is rather obvious. Lots of sesame when initially poured and it took a lot of air to blow the reduction off. Enjoyable nose of citrus and vanilla cream. It's initially crispy but had decent mid-palate presence with some creaminess peaking through. Short and abrupt finish. Fair.

![20251005_170040|375x500](upload://7ANrJ63eQRqhvX1WFs0UXcnRB5Y.jpeg)
**Les Astrelles Bourgogne Les Maladières 2022**
De-stemmed, all oak (10% new). This is extremely feminine: red cherry, raspberry and very floral. Elegant palate, dusty tanins, lively acidity and bright red fruit. Decent finish. Good bottle of Bourgogne.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.raw:
yeah they can be just as solid as the estate wines; have not really come across a bad bottle thus far.  the first vintage of 2017 tercet bourgogne rouge was perhaps even better than the dijon based version.  monopole cras is usually the sweet spot for me but i do slightly prefer the white label wines (if you can get them at a similar price point).  when they are double or so the monopole cras i would say they are not worth it.

edit to update day 2: positive evolution, more open and even some extra volume now.  remarkably stable and clean as a whistle with no degradation from being kept in the fridge overnight.  otto mentioned in his note of a previous vintage that there was some brett and wildness, absolutely not the case here with the 23.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.raw:
I suspect I drink a lot of natural wines without knowing they're actually natural! But this one I did know about:

**Domaine de L'Ecu - Vin de France - Mephisto 2015**

This is a cabernet franc, from a small yield, small production. Quite pale in colour (I meant to take a photo but forgot), a little brown at the edges. The nose was quite appealing, with notes of wild strawberry and plum, but in the mouth, rather odd: diluted and fading blackcurrant, which made me think of a glass of Ribena after its best-before date. I managed half a glass. Pity.

They make impressive Muscadets and most of the whites in their *Copains* series can be quite terrific, but almost all of their *Copains* reds have been pretty disappointing. There have been a few enjoyable exceptions, but even then, these reds are firmly in the **avoid** category for me.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.raw:
[quote="m_ristev, post:632, topic:330008, full:true"] l’ange pinot can be nice. not sure i would age it tho… [/quote] Lol one of the very few wines I've managed to score full 50 points. :sweat_smile: For the sake of lulz, I guess I can post it here, even if the note is already half a decade old:
  • 2017 Domaine de l'Ecu Ange - France, Vin de France (27.10.2020)
    Cuvée MMXVII, Vin de France. Biodynamically farmed Pinot Noir, vinified as whole bunches, fermented spontaneously with indigenous yeasts, macerated for 10 days, aged for 8 months in amphorae, vinified and bottled without any SO2. 13% alcohol. Tasted from a magnum, of which 315 were produced.

    Somewhat cloudy - even slightly murky - yet rather translucent pomegranate red color. Smells of old sweaty gym clothes, sesame seeds and rodent cage - you can guess the wine is going to be heavily mousy without tasting it. The wine is light-bodied, ripe and subtly sweet-toned on the palate with heavily mousy flavors of mousy THP, brambly raspberries, some mousiness, a little bit of sour cherry bitterness, light unclean funky tones, an acetic hint of VA and a mousy touch of rancid nuts. The acidity feels high and the tannins really light and mellow. The finish is quite acid-driven, crunchy and very long with flavors of heavy mousiness, some crunchy cranberries, a little bit of fresh apple and a even some more mousiness.

    A horrible, revolting and undrinkably mousy wine. Many people who got to taste this wine earlier thought it was quite fresh and enjoyable, so I can imagine this wine won't be mousy upon opening, but only after a little while. I got to taste the wine only after a little while and it turned out to be probably the mousiest thing I've ever tasted. This is like Pinot Noir-based extract of THP. Awful stuff. Avoid. (50 points)

posts.name: Michael Blasco
posts.raw:
Just a question here (probably fits best in the natural wine thread!!), how would you characterize brett barnyard vs. mousy? Same "flaw"? Different agents all together?

I drink a fair amount of natural wine and many producers that I enjoy I occasionally get a bretty bottle, but I can't think of the last time I would call something mousy. Maybe I'm just conflating the two and they're degrees of the same character?

E.g. I had a bottle of Baudry Les Granges recently which smelled like it was left in an unclean horse stable during a July heatwave (classic barnyard, many would consider flawed), but opened another bottle last night which had a bit of bretty funk which blew off with a few minutes of air (maybe just call it mousy?)

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.raw:
[quote="m_blasco, post:641, topic:330008"]
Just a question here (probably fits best in the natural wine thread!!), how would you characterize brett barnyard vs. mousy? Same “flaw”? Different agents all together?
[/quote]
Just to add to the responses you’ve already received, it’s not uncommon for Brett and mouse to be present in the same wine, which can complicate the sensory expression. Some of the most heinously mousy wines I have tasted seemed Bretty at first and were then overtaken by mouse after a few minutes. I also do not associate mousy wines with any of the actual rodent experiences I have had. To me it is more in the family of savory flavors - rancid nut butter and speculoos can be similar - but at the end of the day mouse taint tastes like mouse taint in the same way a strawberry tastes like a strawberry - it is what it is.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.raw:
[quote="Rahsaan_M, post:648, topic:330008"]
Same thing with brett, no?
[/quote]

No. Brett stays unchanged.

But it's true that reduction blows off while brett does not.

Although I find it weird to distinguish brett and reduction from each other with this characteristics, since aromatically I don't find much common ground.

[quote="Ben_M_a_n_d_l_e_r, post:647, topic:330008"]
it’s not uncommon for Brett and mouse to be present in the same wine, which can complicate the sensory expression.
[/quote]

This indeed. THP (the chemical compounds responsible for mousiness) can be produced by both brettanomyces and certain lactic acid bacteria, but I think brett is more common. That means that not all bretty wines are mousy, but almost all mousy wines are also bretty.

I think I've never had a wine that didn't have at least some levels of brett if they had mousiness. However, I've had tons of so-called "kettle sours", aka. sour ales inoculated with LAB, that didn't have any brett but were horribly mousy.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.raw:
[quote="Rahsaan_M, post:650, topic:330008"]
Hard to put in words, but the smoky pungent rubbery types of aromas can go either way towards reduction or brett. At least that’s how I’ve been thinking about things.
[/quote]

Fair enough. Rubbery aromas are often obviously reductive to me, but I can imagine the smoky notes can confuse as they are much closer to each other. I personally associate the smoky reductive qualities with something that also has a sulfurous element (flint, gunpowder, matchstick), whereas phenolic smoky aromas have a more smoke-smoky, roasted quality to them. However, that is pretty much splitting hairs and completely understandable if those elements get confused with each other.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.raw:
[quote="Rahsaan_M, post:648, topic:330008"]
Same thing with brett, no?
[/quote]

Not for me. 

With air mousiness will completely overtake your wine. 

With brett it is often the other way around for me. With air the wine can in some cases overtake the brett notes as it opens up, hence the brett is less noticeable if that makes sense.

Now I do have an extreme tolerance of brett and actually enjoy it in many cases, which might influence my view on the subject.

What an awesome wine.
Initially it is good, but not hitting the notes of the best Ganevat's. 

But after a few hours it just gains intensity, overall, and develops a beautiful herbal mint note that adds complexity and a perfect freshness to balance out the subtle nutty oxidative notes. I have only experienced such a mint note in aged Raveneau before.

I have saved half of the bottle for tomorrow to see how it develops further on.

This might be the best kids drawing wine in the world.

![IMG_9961|375x500](upload://vJIifl7OhQCPv1hhz34MP5a9NWW.jpeg)

**Bruno Schueller - Zero Defaut 2015**
Such a seductive nose of honey, curry and marzipan. This cuvee has seen several years of sous voile, giving to that very beautiful depth of both color and complexity. There's a slight residual sugar but a beautiful acidity, of red apples back this up. Finishing off slightly bitter. Very complex. This is amazing, and here in 2025, it has some 15 years left. Going to save my other bottles for a while.

https://leonandsonwine.com/products/2023-domaine-partage-gilles-berlioz-deuse-vin-de-savoie-france#:~:text=The%20Gift%20Shop-,2023%20Domaine%20Partage%20Gilles%20Berlioz%20Deuse%2C%20Vin%20de%20Savoie%2C%20France,Read%20More
posts.cooked:
Drink tons of natural wine rarely post about them.

This was new to me. Asked the somm at COVE for something light to finish the evening. 9.5% alcohol. Super clean and completely ripe. Refreshing, delicious and very enjoyable.

Leon & Son Wine and Spirits

2023 Domaine Partage Gilles Berlioz Deuse, Vin de Savoie, France

La Deuse is a memory of times past when many wines’ alcohol levels struggled to reach 10 degrees. Whole bunches in a sealed vat for two weeks semi-carbonic maceration before pressing. Fermentation and ageing in fibreglass, 20mg/l SO2 total – addition...

Price: USD 65.00

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.raw:
[quote="m_ristev, post:658, topic:330008, full:true"]
not sure why mon pere gets so much attention relative to the other savagnin cuvees.
[/quote]

Probably because for many people it is probably the best one there is?

When we had our first Ganevat tasting close to ten years ago, when I knew quite little about Jura and not much about Ganevat, we had a bunch of wines. VdMP was an extraordinary wine by any and all accounts - not only hands down the best wine in the tasting, but also among the best whites I've ever tasted.

The only wine that came quite close was Cuvée du Pépé - ie. Ganevat's Chardonnay-based "vin jaune" - that was also quite stunning.

posts.name: Mariano Chiaramonte
posts.raw:
![IMG_1802|375x500](upload://kXkybIY4GwVGWJfRFxUPjECEKMd.jpeg)

**2023 Reka Koncz Ora**

I went to LA for the weekend and had this at Anajak Thai (Very highly recommended).
This is my first time trying a skin contact Furmint.
My experience with orange wines in general is that I tend to like them more when is made out of a variety that I don't care much when is vinified as white (ie: Ribolla Gialla, Torrontés, etc).
On the other hand, I don't remember caring much for any skin contact Riesling, Chardonnay or Chenin Blanc.
This Furmint proved to be the exception to my rule, while not the most complex wine out there it proved to be a beautiful balanced and playful wine that went extraordinary well with the diversity of dishes that we had during the course of the night.

posts.name: Maciej Kocan
posts.raw:
Jean-Marie Berrux Le Tetu Blanc 2023

![IMG_0775|432x500](upload://uqxY9cM7hD26Qw7SXSxBvZVO1o2.jpeg)

True to the tenets of the natural winemaking creed, apparently. Clean, faultless, well balanced, maybe a whiff of oxidation? Medium+ acidity, with a touch of vanilla/oak. Salty lemon preserves served on stoneware. With this slightly sweetish, banana like note I often sense in natural whites.
Certainly among my best Chards made in this style.

Lovely 'big' Sauvignon Blanc from Bain. Deep nectarine and bruised fruits and an underlaying oxidisation that he so often does. Always a fan of these to surprise a bit with a different style of Pouilly Fumee.
Not to wait too much very nice now.

**2020 Calalta "Undercover" Merlot**

Organic vineyard about midway between Vicenza and Treviso, Italy. Indigenous yeasts, partial carbonic maceration. Unfiltered, low sulfite. 

Reduction blows off quickly. The nose is savory and slightly wild, with deep black cherry and sweet mint. The palate is juicy with clean fruit and powdery tannin, a fun food-friendly texture, medium body, 12% alcohol.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.raw:
We did a really fun comparison of 2022 Labet Pinot Noir Varron: Massale vs Clonale. ![IMG_2837|375x500](upload://fih1BLdn1YQOc022pRZQmTqPezF.jpeg)
  • 2022 Domaine Labet Pinot Noir Selection Massale Les Varrons - France, Jura, Côtes du Jura (11/17/2025)
    Tasted next to the Selection Clonale. Lovely nose with notes of cherries, red berries, and perhaps a bit more lively feel. Nice acidity with good complexity on the palate, underwood, red fruit, and a bit of savory. Long finish. Great wine! This felt a bit more elegant, refined, and slightly more complex compared to the Clonale, although the overall flavor profile is quite similar. 94+ (94 points)
  • 2022 Domaine Labet Pinot Noir Selection Clonale Les Varrons - France, Jura, Côtes du Jura (11/17/2025)
    Tasted next to the Selection Massale. Maybe a hint less fragrant on the nose with nice notes of red fruit and berries. I felt this had a bit more acidity on the palate but less balance and finesse with more structure, earthiness, and red cherry flavors. Nice finish. Super interesting comparison. Both wines were delicious, but the table unanimously preferred the Massale. (93 points)
Plus we enjoyed some other great Jura wines with excellent Chinese food :heart_eyes: ![IMG_2841|375x500](upload://hrxEg4iBaOpnyruQCSlzdQ9EWoJ.jpeg)
  • 2020 Jean-François Ganevat Chardonnay Les Grands Teppes Vieilles Vignes - France, Jura, Côtes du Jura (11/17/2025)
    Great nose with notes of citrus, white pepper, and tons of minerals. Great acidity on the palate with more lemon, green apple, grapefruit, crushed rocks, and salinity. Long finish. Excellent young Ganevat. This will need 7-10 years to show its best. 95+ (95 points)
  • 2022 Mathieu Allante et Christian Boulanger Pataratte - France, Vin de France (11/17/2025)
    Lovely nose with bright fruit that feels light and pretty and mineral driven. Great acidity on the palate with citrus, green apples, and other orchard fruit. Nice finish. Beautiful (and clean) wine that is young, lively, and precise. Should age well in the mid term, although it doesn't have the complexity of the higher end cuvees. 92+ (92 points)
  • 2014 Jean-François Ganevat Julien en Billat - France, Jura, Côtes du Jura (11/17/2025)
    Nice nose that feels Burgundian with a bit of funkiness. Notes of strawberries, red cherries, and underwood on the nose. Nice acidity with more strawberry flavors, flowers, cherries, earth, and pine needles on the palate. Nice finish that lingers. This is a gorgeous wine with nice complexity. Can't believe it has that much going on with only 11% ABV. (93 points)

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.raw:
I usually don’t drink natural wine, but went to a natural wine place with friends who’re into natural wine a few  weeks ago in Tokyo.

![Sorry, not the best picture|375x500, 100%](upload://iSMVcB9fve57ldTWqCuCsDXEf8b.jpeg)

The place doesn’t have a wine list like many other natural wine bars, and the staff presents a few bottles based on the style of wine customers request.

We started off with Champagne. I don’t remember the producer - does anyone recognize the label? Anyways, it is 100% Pinot with a pinkish hue. Expressive with ripe tree fruits, a bit aggressive bubbles and a touch of tannic structure on the palate. Quite grower in style, and as it was kept open towards to the end of the dinner, the riper, cooked apple notes emerged. This is where things got interesting because I don’t like that sweeter cooked apple aromatic and I basically see the wine lost tension and freshness, but all 3 friends loved it and saw it as the wine gaining richness and weight. Overall a good wine, but not memorable and I felt I can find a myriads of other grower champagnes that taste exactly like this.

Next up we asked for a light fresh white and the lineup of options presented was mostly Loire. I knew Bernaudeau even as someone who’s not into natural wine, and I insisted to try it. Once the owner saw my excitement for the wine, the owner told us they have other cuvées if we wanted them but Les Ongles is most open when young. We went with Les Ongles, and I had quite a bit of expectation. The wine had a touch of reductive note upon opening and appeared closed, but it became open as it warmed up. It just had a particular salted meat aroma that reminded me of pancetta (perhaps due to some herbal notes), but otherwise it tasted pretty archetypical for Chenin and it’s hard for me to tell this is natural wine in blind. Mix of citrus and tree fruits, sharp acid and that waxy texture. Good wine and doesn’t taste natty at all, but also not impressive. We probably paid like $100 for a bottle at a restaurant, which I think is a fair deal, but I can’t comprehend why folks pay $150+ in retail. Probably I should try a few more bottles before I give a personal verdict though.

Then we asked for a richer white and the owner presented a bunch of Jura. I only recognized Labet but my friend wanted to try des Marnes Blanches’ Savagnin. The wine has a light golden hue with intense aromatics of biological aging and fresh tree fruits. Really good balance - fresh yet complex. This isn’t again funky at all to me, and is very correct. I’m not even sure if this producer is natural leaning particularly.

The Savagnin was about to be the winner of the night for me, but the owner came back with glasses of wine in a deep golden hue. He liked us opening a bottle after a bottle, and offered Labet Vin Jaune 2016 they had an open bottle of. Much more complexity and weight than the previous Jura wine. Even though this has less of a fresh fruits, the extra acidity makes up for it. Intense, complex and seductive. By far the winner of the night. Both of the Jura wine went fantastically with the roasted guinea fowl dish with beurre blanc sauce (or maybe it was Vin Jaune sauce).

Lastly, we asked for a red. At this point, the owner showed a few bottles, but literally told us “tell me what you want to drink and I’ll bring them if I have them in the onsite cellar”. After some discussion, we decided to go with one of the few bottles presented, Le Fil de Soi. This was the only disappointment last night. The wine smelled floral and correct upon opening, but it had a bit of residual bubbles on the palate. As it got air, it became quite funky with a bit of mousiness in my opinion, and this is not a correct wine to me. That said, my friends who drink natural wine regularly didn’t seem to mind much though they were not excited about the wine either.

Overall, a great experience to try a few natural wine at reasonable prices, and this experience confirms I should drink more Jura. Though I’m not converted to be a natural wine drinker, I’m genuinely appreciative to the restaurants like this which are willing to offer and share rare wine to those who are passionate and care about wine. I think this type of restaurants/bars are much more common among places that are natural leaning, compared to say bars that specialize in Burgundy.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
[quote="TakaoS, post:713, topic:330008"]
it’s hard for me to tell this is natural wine in blind
[/quote]

We're not gathering in here to praise wine that tastes 'natty'. Instead it's a place, for me at least, to talk about and inspire to drink wines that are created of just grapes. Something apparently a lot of growers find it hard to do without a myriad of help. And so many others prove is difficult without…

When that is said, I'm happy to see you liked the Empreinte by Marnes Blanches. Even more so the Labet VJ. Simply amazing. Marnes Blanches hasn't released any oxy wines for a while now, but they just released a range of wines that had 3 years of elevage Ouille. Curious to try.

What was the vintage of the Les Ongles?

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.raw:
[quote="MadsW, post:715, topic:330008"]
We’re not gathering in here to praise wine that tastes ‘natty’.
[/quote]

Fair point. It’s interesting though, most of my friends who’re into natural wine are Japanese, and most of them think there’s a certain quality that they look for in a natural wine that conventional wines typically don’t possess, and they say that in a positive way. Probably “natty” isn’t the proper translation because that’s used negatively more often than not.

That said, I’m overall sounding a bit critical toward natural wine just because of faulty wines I encounter from time to time. I like many wines that happen to be natural, but it’s a bit hard for me not to be skeptical when the low is really low for the price I have to pay.

They told us they just received the wine so I’m pretty sure it was 2023 though I didn’t take a note.

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
![IMG_4974|375x500](upload://4cjBQ9UCCg30TIoA8f9T6NQwecW.jpeg)


2011 Milos Plavac (Mali)

A bit mute on the fruits on the first day (PnP) and start of second, thereafter it really opened up. 

Citrus, sage, licorice, plums, raspberries and even wild strawberries. Tannins are still very present, hasn’t soften much (much less than I would have guessed). 

Focused and energetic with a very long finish, chalky salinity. Drinking very well with the right food (avoid sweet or spicy). 

Impressive how well a “humble” entry wine keeps up and shines at least as well as a fine single vineyard Barbaresco (2015 Musso Pora) from a good producer followed side by side.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.raw:
Marnes Blanches, Au Rettin Chardonnay, 2022. 

34 month in demi-muids (ouillé)

Slightly muted nose initially. The fruit slowly grows with air (over two days). Pineapple and mature sweet apples. There are lemon and salt notes as expected, but this is one of the most "salty" wines I've ever tried! While the fruit is more in the background it already shows some brioche, caramel and honey. Deep and delicious taste.
High acidity, but no noticeable VA as in many white Jura. Very pure in that sense. It is really good, but hopefully a few years will help it open up a bit more from an aromatic perspective. Great potential here.

For 40€ a no brainer. Excited to try the other two "long elevage" cuvées.

![1000004374|375x500](upload://bSjAOEM3hJxnn65RahoXHaFo4iA.jpeg)
![1000004373|375x500](upload://t2NkAE3qqxQ2r4jhDnhMDfMjlii.jpeg)

posts.name: Bennett Tyson
posts.raw:
A few wines for thanksgiving
Carsten Saalwaecter Weissburgunder 21 - No formal notes but this was very balanced with riper fruit, acid, and some yeast? lees? to help add complexity and a little structure.
Envinate Benje Tinto 17 - Had bad luck with TCA and their current release reds, but this was terrific. Light crunchy red fruit, a little irony, a little herbal, this was a terrific lighter red to go with the meal. 
Lena Singer-Fischer BDB 20 - great way to start the evening, this was a little more fruit forward and acid driven without feeling austere. Autolytic notes were in great balance, the small dosage made a big difference to round things out

posts.name: Edward Thompson
posts.raw:
![IMG_4781|375x500](upload://qWuGT8zhaAvPYbeOhHAfID43pPd.jpeg)
Absolutely mind blowing. There is so much tension and density in this wine. Having gone through malo and being slightly oxidative in nature, it completely subverts expectations of a sauv blanc from the Loire, or anywhere really. It is massive both on the nose and the palette - there is definitely some small amount of residual sugar here despite the 13.5% alcohol. I get notes of citrus peel, tropical fruit, pastry cream & candied nuts. Such a unique expression.

posts.raw:
![IMG_0230.HEIC|375x500](upload://AhlpFm1DbUJIqz8SSQTpGEPIdjj.jpeg)

**Les Grandes Vignes - La Varenne du Poirier 2021**

Very savagnin like nose of honey, pear and sesame leaf. Not so odd given the genetic relationship of Savagnin and Chenin. The drink is moderately long and is comprised of green apple, honey and a salinic touch. The acidity is on the strong side, but the ripeness of the fruit is bothering me a little bit. It's not neither here nor there in Chenin typicity but very well made and interesting none the less. 

For 20€ in EU it's quite the bargain.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.raw:
![IMG_20251224_203139280|375x500](upload://fYKVEOJQV6c6fO3g96D33uqrSfl.jpeg)

i recall tom hill posted a rather pessimistic note about this wine a while back.  having never tried this gruner cuvée i was really curious to give it a taste.  in my experience evan's wines have continued to improve over the years and at this stage they are quite clean and stable with even a slightly serious structure.  this wine is no different and opens with an effusive nose of classic gruner herbal notes and heady satsuma fruit.  lithe on the palate at 11.1% alcohol yet stains the tongue with thick apricot fruit.  the wine culminates with a tangy lemon edge accompanied with the delicate tannin of a black tea.  with air the nose becomes distinctly floral like an aesop soap.  delicious and highly drinkable.
posts.cooked:

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.raw:
I drink a ton of natural wine but rarely post on it.  I love shopping for wine in Buffalo during my annual once a year return home because I find and try wines I would never buy elsewhere. 

I saw a bottle with a beautiful label that piqued my interest 2023 Jean-Paul Brun Terres Dorées Bourgone.  I have never seen a Brun from Bourgone. The wine was a whopping $27 before discount so I took a flyer.  

WOW! 

What a wine. This is what I love about natural wine, lively, energetic, vivid, juicy and just overall delicous.  Dark cherry fruit, soaring aromatics and such an absolute pleasure to drink with a slight chill.

If you know of a better Pinot at this price point I need to know. 

This is the importer write up on the 24:

https://www.bowlerwine.com/wine-or-spirit/pinot-noir-bourgogne/2024

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.raw: First night in the Mosel!  Ordered a 19 Melsheimer Lentum, my first time with this wine.  This a fully natural, no SO2 or very little, extended barrel aging wine. 2019 is the current release.  A few bottles of the 18 were truly profound and a couple were off.  This smelled funky on opening but quickly blew off.  What emerged was a fascinating interpretation of riesling that was unique but still undeniably from the Mosel.
posts.cooked: First night in the Mosel! Ordered a 19 Melsheimer Lentum, my first time with this wine. This a fully natural, no SO2 or very little, extended barrel aging wine. 2019 is the current release. A few bottles of the 18 were truly profound and a couple were off. This smelled funky on opening but quickly blew off. What emerged was a fascinating interpretation of riesling that was unique but still undeniably from the Mosel.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_0360|375x500](upload://nkp7zi69NVbceIoSK2jyhEeaSzN.jpeg)

Quiet New Years eve w. my partner at the local french inspired bistrot. Sadly, I am still being referred to as 'the regular who loves macerated wine', despite it actually being her who loves it and can't dine without. I mean, it complements everything from fish to red meats, but I'm a little bored with it…
There was some Bini Serraghia from '21 & '16 but in a country that doesn't take back mousy bottles it's a risky bet, and the Schueller Edelzwicker 2020 is a VA mess. So the Ligas it was.

**Domaine Ligas - Lambda Assyrtiko 2022**
Herby, peachy and salty. Those are the three words to describe it. The nose presents itself with ripe stone fruits and a touch of herbs like sage and a bit of thyme. The acidity is on the rather fresh and punchy side without it venturing into VA land, and the tannins aren't overpowering. It's not the longest finish in the world, but a very typical one that has a slight saline touch. Generally a happy glass.

Happy NY

Oxymore Songe Eveille 2024
It has all what you hope for in a clean natural Jura Savagnin + Chardonnay blend.

Nicolas Carmarans Maximus 2021 100% Fer Servadou (?)
But a Minimus would better describe the drinking pleasure. Scratchy tannins, vinegar notes, a trace of fizz. Hard core fans will see it differently.

A New Year resolution: less natty wines, back to the classics.

Very complex, very lively, and just incredibly engaging. What a wine! Unbelievable that a natural white wine from 1996 is so alive and needs this much air.

posts.name: Maciej Kocan
posts.raw:
Cyril Fhal Clos du Rouge George Cuvee du Patron 2022
![IMG_0872|375x500](upload://ewcQ7ARRTajgRcRErPvyT0rgxSp.jpeg)


Grenache, and depending on whom you trust, Carignan or Mourvedre (I suspect the former). It has everything that polarizes: scratchy tannins, funk, a faint vinegery note. Good acidity. Dark fruit, peat, yeast. 
It looks like the wines have some following. Or maybe it’s selection bias: the wines get predominantly picked by hardcore fans of the genre. If you prefer round, well behaved wines look elsewhere.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.raw:
I tried the 2020 Vieilles Vignes last summer - and really quite enjoyed its fresh, cool profile.

Well, I just had a fun time at Les Caves du Panthéon in Paris, which I suspect lovers of natural wines will already know of. I’ve not been there for many years but my wife has been buying me regular birthday presents there (most of which I’ve really enjoyed). I had a great chat with the owner and filled a carton of six with some new wines to try, all natural (I believe), which I shall report on in due course. I don’t think there’s a website and the range changes all the time anyway, but it’s quite wonderful - like Ali Baba’s cave. I felt like I did as a whippersnapper in a sweet shop!

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.raw:
![1000004931|666x500](upload://qsN6kVlZIQayGJD7lB7xBFiojmG.jpeg)

a few natural Jura’s last night. All showing tremendously well.

I have heard about variance on Labet’s 18’s, but I have only had good bottles. This 18 Lias was reductive Jura heaven. Storing these types of wines correctly is probably even more important. At release two good friends and I bought around 50-60 bottles of the 18’s and not a single one have been off or felt old.

2019 Aux Bois from Marnes Blanches have started to develop a bit of butter and caramel notes. But acidity is on point and you get all the lemon and salt notes you want. It shows great now, but from experience I know this is at its best. They don’t age well past this age most of the time.

My first long elevage from Labet. 2015. Seven years ouillé. 15,8% abv. What the fuck 😅

I heard a lot about these wines and how oxi some of them shows. So we treaded it like an oxi wine and drank it while eating various cheeses. That was a good choice. It was super oxi, but the depth in taste was amazing! A lot of salty, cheesy and nutty notes.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.raw:
![IMG_0453|375x500](upload://rOoYLgPxkOcHk0u1VB2Ef5sgOx0.jpeg)

**Julien Petitjean. Domaine de la Roseraie. Cuvee Artemis 2020. B. 746 of 870.**

This is quite a nice surprise. The cork, however, was very wet, and it’s doubtful the wine would have lived to 2030, so I hope that despite the dogmatic natural approach of the domaine, they will start using DIAM. 

Stone fruits, pomelo, and chamomile are the dominant scents. The viscosity is a little oily, probably from extended elevage. Still, the wine doesn’t seem over-oaked or anything like that, because the acidity is quite lively and plays well against the oak. The palate is quite serious with Meyer lemon, ripe peach, and just a touch of vanilla. It’s somewhat giving me a riper version of Lamys Chataigniers. I paid 80,000 KRW, which should be equivalent to something like 45€. For that, it might be a little expensive. Definitely picking something up if I find it at a discount in Europe.

posts.raw:

[quote="Phil_T_r_o_t_t_e_r, post:9036, topic:285895"]
Also, this in restaurant:

![20260131_195133](upload://esY6Bk6I2UjhFf8rFTWXBrHikPg)

**Vignoble de la Bauge ÉlémentAir 2019**
I hadn’t heard of this (but then again I don’t follow Quebec wines too closely, mea culpa). From the Somm, this is négoce with the grapes coming from Ontario. They didn’t intend to make a sous-voile but they got voile when in foudre. The winemaker knows Fanfan so he called him and asked what he should do. And so 2018 ÉlémentAir was born. 100% Frontenac Blanc, 5 years sous voile. Beautiful nose with bright lemon, fresh walnuts, a little smoke and florals. On the palate, the oxidative profile is not in your face. The nuttiness and curry twang, acts as a backdrop to a fairly tight and acidic wine. Very well made with the sous-voile adding length and complexity. I see this as long-lived. If blinded, is like to think I would have guessed Jura Savagnin blend.

![20260131_190916](upload://bJrkikFhtnx1GJv3CISlsK4bsXv)

**Stella Crinita Omaggio Pet-Nat**
100% Cabernet Franc. Funky little beast that sees 4 days maceration. Deep red, abundant bubble and mucho taninns! The tannic structure, vegetable/ savoury profile is surprising but enjoyable. I see this as a restaurant only wine with the Somm trying to sell some to curious diners based on unexpected pairings. This was 18$ a glass at the restaurant and I now see it sells for 18$ a bottle in the US. I was bamboozled.

Finished with a Génépi du Trièves from Jérémy Bricka. I should have been told this wasn’t really a Génépi. No added sugar, this drinks like a Gin with Génépi florals understated.
[/quote]

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.raw: Posting some natural (or at least natural-adjacent) Juras I've enjoyed with many of my fellow SF compadres over the past six months. Most of these wines were not drunk together. **WHITE WINES** 2020 Jean-François Ganevat Chardonnay Cuvée Florine: Expressive aromas of lemon and white flowers on a nose that is just a touch lifted. Sleek, silky and subtly textured with punchy acidity that is mouth-watering, but already fairly well integrated. Juicy white fruit with a grace not of bitter lemon peel on the palate. There's warm spices and a subtle note of salinity and vanilla on the finish. Already very approachable, in contrast to the '20 Grands Teppes VV, which could use more time. 2018 Jean-François Ganevat Grusse en Billat: The nose is a little more subtle and stony in comparison to the '20 Florine. There's tart acidity on the opening attack that is quite firm but already well integrated. Much more mineral and saltier, you can taste the wet rocks on the palate. Razor sharp, the notes of spice and vanilla from the oak are already quite integrated into the palate. In comparison with the '20 Florine, this has much more mid-palate intensity. 2017 Anne et Jean-François Ganevat Savagnin Arbois Sous Voile: Strong oxidative aromas of roasted nuts, honey, and a slight sting of wood varnish on the nose. The palate is very tart and lemon-forward from the strong acidity with the oxidative, sherry-adjacent flavors adding a layer of complexity onto the palate. Very powerful, but also very light on its feet and super elegant, as if its floating over the palate. My first time drinking a sous voile white from Ganevat, and it is really good! NV Jean-François Ganevat Macvin du Jura Vieux: A blend of mostly Chardonnay with some Savagnin. Quite oxidative on the nose with the heat from the fortification also noticeable. Some honeyed notes on the palate, and it finishes with a hint of sweetness with some heat and bite. Despite the alcohol, it comes across as light and finessed (for a fortified dessert wine). 2020 Jean-François Ganevat Chardonnay Les Grands Teppes Vieilles Vignes: Lots of aromatic floral aromas on the nose with tart citrus and salinity on the palate accompanied by a vibrant, lively acidity. However, this is still a little tightly coiled. It could use a couple more years in the cellar to unwind. But, lots of great potential. 2022 Mathieu Allante et Christian Boulanger Pataratte: Really delicious and approachable right now. Definitely natural, with plenty of that lemony, acid-driven, feral quality you get from Jura Chardonnay, but clean and bright without any flaws. 2005 Jean-François Ganevat Côtes du Jura Les Vignes de Mon Père: Wow, absolutely electric acidity that just persists from the initial attack throughout the long, long finish. The palate is infused with lemon, salt, and crushed rock, and texturally, this is quite fine and seamless, with a lot more polish and finesse than the feral quality that Jura whites can sometimes have. Razor sharp with incredible tension and length. Phenomenal wine. From magnum. 2012 Jean-François Ganevat Côtes du Jura Cuvée Marguerite: Another delicious and scintillating Jura Chardonnay (Melon-Queue-Rouge), with tremendous acid, citrus, salt, and minerality on the palate. At this point, texturally, the wine has shed the feral, slightly crunchy edge that young Jura whites can have; it's all seamlessly sleek and spherical at this point. Just showed beautifully. From magnum. 2020 Domaine Labet Fleur de Savagnin: Big, bold, in-your-face rendition of Jura Savagnin with lots of yellow fruit and citrus on the palate. A bit more rough-n-tumble than the Ganevat wines, but this is also a lot younger. Great acidity, long finish, perhaps a little hint of vanilla from the barrel and a bit of oxidative nuttiness, but nonetheless, a heavy-hitter of a Jura white. 2019 Jean-François Ganevat Côtes du Jura Les Varrons Vieilles Vignes: An aromatically lifted nose while the palate is quite crisp and silky with mouth-watering acidity that is already well-integrated, but lively enough to compel you to drink more. I'm getting flavors of tart lemon, white fruit, and a hint of lavender and cilantro. The finish is long, but subtle with just a touch of natty volatility and funk. Very light on its feet and well balanced. Not getting much vanilla or creaminess here: this is quite pure and the oak-influence is barely a whisper. While it does not have the concentration and energy of the '19 Chalasses, it also seems more approachable now with its seamless, elegant texture. This is cloudy, acid-driven lemonade for grown-ups. 2022 Tissot (Bénédicte et Stéphane / André et Mireille) Arbois Les Bruyères: A bit hefty and a touch polished, but balanced with nice acidity, a hint of oxidative funkiness, and just a kiss of oak to add a layer of complexity to the fresh-tasting citrus and white on the palate. **RED WINES** 2014 Jean-François Ganevat Julien en Billat: This is a lovely example of a natural red wine with the classic hallmarks of semi-carbonic maceration: beautiful wild strawberries and watermelon on the nose with hints of green from the stems. Acidity is quite lively with hints of herbs and black pepper seasoning the crunchy red fruit. Juicy, fun, and dynamic on the palate (and low alcohol, which is a nice bonus). Pinot Noir. 2023 Jean-François Ganevat Pinot Noir En Billat: Wild strawberries, violets, and herbs on the nose. The palate has subtle red, brambly fruit, great acidity, and white pepper with bitter herbs. Light and silky in a carbonic way. Very tart on the finish. 2023 Domaine Morel - Les Pieds Sur Terre Poulsard En Boutasse: Pretty wild strawberries and florals on the nose, but also a bit lifted. Some volatility frames the delicate red fruit and herbs, but I would say it adds interest rather than detracts. Very refreshing and pure, though not overly complex and thought-provoking. A nice, glou-glou bistro wine. 2022 Domaine Labet Pinot Noir Selection Massale Les Varrons: No formal note, but this was really refined and elegant. Beautiful, wild strawberries on the nose intermingling with those distinctive floral, spiced, and slightly stemmy herbal aromas you get from whole clusters. Palate has juicy red fruit, acidity that is already well-integrated with just a touch of joyous crunch. A really nice example of natural pinot noir. 2022 Domaine Labet Pinot Noir Selection Clonale Les Varrons: I had this next to the selection massale, and while it shares a lot of similar characteristics (herbal, peppery, floral aromas, red-fruited palate), the acidity was a little punchier and texturally the wine was not as refined. Still quite good, but definitely a step down from the selection massale. 2020 Les Granges Paquenesses Côtes du Jura Le Plou: Surprisingly dense and ripe for a Poulsard, I think the solar-nature of the vintage did this wine no favors. On opening, there is a lot of CO2 and crunchiness on the palate, but that does blow off with time, giving the wine a sleeker, more approachable texture. Interestingly herbal and dark-fruited, with a pleasing, subtle bitter grip on the finish from the tannins, but this seems just a touch too big and high-octane for me. It doesn't have the delicacy and weightlessness I normally expect from a Jura Poulsard.
posts.cooked:
Posting some natural (or at least natural-adjacent) Juras I’ve enjoyed with many of my fellow SF compadres over the past six months. Most of these wines were not drunk together.

WHITE WINES

2020 Jean-François Ganevat Chardonnay Cuvée Florine: Expressive aromas of lemon and white flowers on a nose that is just a touch lifted. Sleek, silky and subtly textured with punchy acidity that is mouth-watering, but already fairly well integrated. Juicy white fruit with a grace not of bitter lemon peel on the palate. There’s warm spices and a subtle note of salinity and vanilla on the finish. Already very approachable, in contrast to the '20 Grands Teppes VV, which could use more time.

2018 Jean-François Ganevat Grusse en Billat: The nose is a little more subtle and stony in comparison to the '20 Florine. There’s tart acidity on the opening attack that is quite firm but already well integrated. Much more mineral and saltier, you can taste the wet rocks on the palate. Razor sharp, the notes of spice and vanilla from the oak are already quite integrated into the palate. In comparison with the '20 Florine, this has much more mid-palate intensity.

2017 Anne et Jean-François Ganevat Savagnin Arbois Sous Voile: Strong oxidative aromas of roasted nuts, honey, and a slight sting of wood varnish on the nose. The palate is very tart and lemon-forward from the strong acidity with the oxidative, sherry-adjacent flavors adding a layer of complexity onto the palate. Very powerful, but also very light on its feet and super elegant, as if its floating over the palate. My first time drinking a sous voile white from Ganevat, and it is really good!

NV Jean-François Ganevat Macvin du Jura Vieux: A blend of mostly Chardonnay with some Savagnin. Quite oxidative on the nose with the heat from the fortification also noticeable. Some honeyed notes on the palate, and it finishes with a hint of sweetness with some heat and bite. Despite the alcohol, it comes across as light and finessed (for a fortified dessert wine).

2020 Jean-François Ganevat Chardonnay Les Grands Teppes Vieilles Vignes: Lots of aromatic floral aromas on the nose with tart citrus and salinity on the palate accompanied by a vibrant, lively acidity. However, this is still a little tightly coiled. It could use a couple more years in the cellar to unwind. But, lots of great potential.

2022 Mathieu Allante et Christian Boulanger Pataratte: Really delicious and approachable right now. Definitely natural, with plenty of that lemony, acid-driven, feral quality you get from Jura Chardonnay, but clean and bright without any flaws.

2005 Jean-François Ganevat Côtes du Jura Les Vignes de Mon Père: Wow, absolutely electric acidity that just persists from the initial attack throughout the long, long finish. The palate is infused with lemon, salt, and crushed rock, and texturally, this is quite fine and seamless, with a lot more polish and finesse than the feral quality that Jura whites can sometimes have. Razor sharp with incredible tension and length. Phenomenal wine. From magnum.

2012 Jean-François Ganevat Côtes du Jura Cuvée Marguerite: Another delicious and scintillating Jura Chardonnay (Melon-Queue-Rouge), with tremendous acid, citrus, salt, and minerality on the palate. At this point, texturally, the wine has shed the feral, slightly crunchy edge that young Jura whites can have; it’s all seamlessly sleek and spherical at this point. Just showed beautifully. From magnum.

2020 Domaine Labet Fleur de Savagnin: Big, bold, in-your-face rendition of Jura Savagnin with lots of yellow fruit and citrus on the palate. A bit more rough-n-tumble than the Ganevat wines, but this is also a lot younger. Great acidity, long finish, perhaps a little hint of vanilla from the barrel and a bit of oxidative nuttiness, but nonetheless, a heavy-hitter of a Jura white.

2019 Jean-François Ganevat Côtes du Jura Les Varrons Vieilles Vignes: An aromatically lifted nose while the palate is quite crisp and silky with mouth-watering acidity that is already well-integrated, but lively enough to compel you to drink more. I’m getting flavors of tart lemon, white fruit, and a hint of lavender and cilantro. The finish is long, but subtle with just a touch of natty volatility and funk. Very light on its feet and well balanced. Not getting much vanilla or creaminess here: this is quite pure and the oak-influence is barely a whisper. While it does not have the concentration and energy of the '19 Chalasses, it also seems more approachable now with its seamless, elegant texture. This is cloudy, acid-driven lemonade for grown-ups.

2022 Tissot (Bénédicte et Stéphane / André et Mireille) Arbois Les Bruyères: A bit hefty and a touch polished, but balanced with nice acidity, a hint of oxidative funkiness, and just a kiss of oak to add a layer of complexity to the fresh-tasting citrus and white on the palate.

RED WINES

2014 Jean-François Ganevat Julien en Billat: This is a lovely example of a natural red wine with the classic hallmarks of semi-carbonic maceration: beautiful wild strawberries and watermelon on the nose with hints of green from the stems. Acidity is quite lively with hints of herbs and black pepper seasoning the crunchy red fruit. Juicy, fun, and dynamic on the palate (and low alcohol, which is a nice bonus). Pinot Noir.

2023 Jean-François Ganevat Pinot Noir En Billat: Wild strawberries, violets, and herbs on the nose. The palate has subtle red, brambly fruit, great acidity, and white pepper with bitter herbs. Light and silky in a carbonic way. Very tart on the finish.

2023 Domaine Morel - Les Pieds Sur Terre Poulsard En Boutasse: Pretty wild strawberries and florals on the nose, but also a bit lifted. Some volatility frames the delicate red fruit and herbs, but I would say it adds interest rather than detracts. Very refreshing and pure, though not overly complex and thought-provoking. A nice, glou-glou bistro wine.

2022 Domaine Labet Pinot Noir Selection Massale Les Varrons: No formal note, but this was really refined and elegant. Beautiful, wild strawberries on the nose intermingling with those distinctive floral, spiced, and slightly stemmy herbal aromas you get from whole clusters. Palate has juicy red fruit, acidity that is already well-integrated with just a touch of joyous crunch. A really nice example of natural pinot noir.

2022 Domaine Labet Pinot Noir Selection Clonale Les Varrons: I had this next to the selection massale, and while it shares a lot of similar characteristics (herbal, peppery, floral aromas, red-fruited palate), the acidity was a little punchier and texturally the wine was not as refined. Still quite good, but definitely a step down from the selection massale.

2020 Les Granges Paquenesses Côtes du Jura Le Plou: Surprisingly dense and ripe for a Poulsard, I think the solar-nature of the vintage did this wine no favors. On opening, there is a lot of CO2 and crunchiness on the palate, but that does blow off with time, giving the wine a sleeker, more approachable texture. Interestingly herbal and dark-fruited, with a pleasing, subtle bitter grip on the finish from the tannins, but this seems just a touch too big and high-octane for me. It doesn’t have the delicacy and weightlessness I normally expect from a Jura Poulsard.

Full Body 2022 by Fabien Henriot.

Fresh, tense, and intriguing Savagnin from Jura, France. The color is a slightly hazy medium gold. Was pretty linear at first with aromas of apples and confit lemons before showing more tropical fruit with notes of mango, grapefruit, and pineapple. Crisp acidity on the palate balanced out nicely with some fullness. Lemon, pineapple, minerals, and a hint of salt. Medium length finish. 

This was my first encounter with one of Fabien Henriot’s wines and it was definitely a fun Savagnin. Far from anything I would have expected. I’m looking forward to trying his other Jura cuvées in the future. 

This Savagnin is Fabien's cuve version and was aged in dame-jeanne for two years I believe. He only produced 44 bottles of this micro-cuvée.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.raw:
**Alexandre Bain - L d'Ange VDF 2022**

Formerly a Pouilly-Fumé, now a Vin de France. I don't know if he opted out or was excluded, but it certainly doesn't taste like a P-F! 
On the nose, vibrant aromas of apricot, honey and yellow peach, then quite a rich attack of the same fruits, before a much drier wave of lemon, and a slightly oxidative finish. Not really for me. 
Very much an acquired taste, his wines, of which I've tasted four in the last few months, always flirt with oxidised flavours, and two of the bottles tried were too oxidised to enjoy at all. I liked the energy and originality but could have done with more freshness.
I know he has his fans - each to their own!

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.raw:
• 2020 Domaine Nicolas Faure Nuits St. Georges Les Herbues - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Nuits St. Georges (2/10/2026)
    Nice nose of dark fruit, herbs, a just a hint of green stems. The palate is a bit plummy, but the texture is so sleek and silky, which finishes with just a hint of charcoal. Nice acidity and lift, and well-judged use of whole clusters. Really well done and enjoyable.

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 2018 Jean-François Ganevat Trousseau Plein Sud - France, Jura, Côtes du Jura (2/10/2026)
    Bright, but almost pinkish robe in the glass. Beautifully floral on the nose with aromas of crushed wild strawberries. The palate is also joyous crunchy red fruit girded with just enough VA and acidity to provide liveliness to the palate, but not over-the-top funk. Really a peak natty, glou-glou red in all the best ways.

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 2017 Daniel Bouland Côte de Brouilly Cuvée Mélanie - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Côte de Brouilly (2/10/2026)
    I'm not normally the biggest Daniel Bouland fan, but this tasted pretty correct and lively. Fruit was a bit dark, but nicely crunchy with just a hint of green and sous bois to lend some complexity to the palate. Pretty good. From Jeroboam.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.raw:
![IMG_0674|375x500](upload://y8MWe8hu1zJwbwWtlDt4UEpm86x.jpeg)

**Les jardins de la Martiniere - X/RG14 (2014)**

Old plot tended to by Xavier Caillard, but recently acquired by a japanese investor group, consulted by Hirotake Ooka. Have some knowledge of the cuvees by now and the wines are great, but stupidly difficult to get by. 

Deep ruby color with a good clarity, with a ever sooo slight bricking hue around the edges. The nose is very strong ripe cherry, leather, sous bois and a bit of tobacco. Here’s also a sweet or candied berry component which was also present in the other Ko-Ki cuvée I tried. The wine is dominated by dark fruit, pencil shavings and just a tiny amount of pyrazines compared to the likes of Lenoir. The acidity is a little downplayed and I would say that it’s just entered its drinking window and will stay there some 7 years. Beautiful effort. QPR to the auction prices is doubtful. Paid 60€ for this and drinking well for that price.

2019 Strohmeier Weiss No. 10 (70% pinot blanc / 30% chard) for the NYC blizzard 

First time trying this producer and I was pleasantly surprised. Very pretty nose, good mix of savory and sweet, sort of a preserved lemon vibe. Acid still very vibrant, missing a bit of structure for me in the midpalate but ends on a pleasantly bitter black sesame note. A little bit of mouse after 6 or so hours but we were on our last glass by then. Drinking well now, doubtful it will improve with further aging.

I am really loving the Soyard wines.

2022 Dirty Gold Las Cimas Vineyard Zinfandel 

This is a new(ish) label from Evan Lewandowski focusing on more traditional single varietal/vineyard expressions than he makes under Ruth Lewandowski. So far the Dirty Gold wines (this is my 2nd) have been some of my favorite wines I've tried from him. This zin showed a beautiful balance of jammy, classically California fruit character with a bright mineral levity. At first it could almost be mistaken for cru Beaujolais, but the mid-palate deepens into an earthy heft which felt very true to a Sonoma zin. Highly recommend trying these if you can find them.
